Arctic high pressure, associated with record or near record surface pressure, settled into the mid Missouri River Valley during the early morning on the 7th. The combination of arctic air with actual air temperatures from 5 below to 5 above zero, and winds of 15 to 30 mph with gusts to near 40 mph created wind chill values of 15 to 25 below zero starting in the early morning on the 7th continuing through the early afternoon in many areas.

Snowfall of 1 to 3 inches combined with winds gusting to over 40 mph to cause near zero visibilities in blowing snow for a few hours in extreme northeast Nebraska from the evening of January 3rd to just after midnight on January 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 1 to 3 inches while winds were gusting to over 40 mph caused widespread near zero visibilities during much of the evening of January 3rd. Travel in open areas was temporarily brought to a halt.

Snow accumulating up to 7 inches was accompanied by blowing snow as a result of northwest winds 20 to 35 mph. The main impact of the storm was to cause difficulties in weekend travel for those who did not delay their travel plans. The storm began on January 31st and continued into the new month through much of February 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 4 to 7 inches, including 5.1 inches at Wakefield. Accompanying northwest winds gusting to 35 mph caused areas of blowing snow, reducing visibility to 1/4 mile at a few places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed early this Wednesday afternoon and evening, and predominantly produced tornadoes and flash flooding. With no cap in place, scattered thunderstorms began developing as early as the noon hour over north central Kansas. Most storms rapidly became supercellular. These supercells rapidly lifted north into south central Nebraska and began producing severe weather by 330 p.m. CDT. Most of the tornadoes occurred over Nuckolls and Thayer counties. One tornado did occur over Adams county and a brief spin-up also occurred over Hall county. Eventually, an MCS developed. The leading edge of the cold pool becoming stationary. New storms repeatedly developed over north central Kansas and lifted northeast along the boundary, through Nuckolls and Thayer counties and further downstream into southeastern Nebraska. Cell training produced a swath of 5-11 inches of rain over portions of Nuckolls and Thayer counties. The highest amount reported was 10.91 inches just west of Hebron. This amount of rainfall in 24 hours typically occurs once in 1,000 years at any given location, or stated another way, has a .1% chance of occurrence in a given 24 hour period. Cooperative observers in Hebron and Superior reported 8.59 and 7.13 inches, respectively. These were the highest 24-hour rainfall amounts ever recorded at these locations, dating back to 1893. Flash flooding damaged roads and some bridges were washed out. U.S. Highways 81 and 136 were closed for a time due to the flooding.||A complex low pressure system extended from the Canadian Prairies into the western United States. These storms developed in the warm sector, far away from any discernible surface boundaries. However, an axis of confluence was present over north central Kansas. A deep upper-level trough was over the western U.S., with a ridge over the East. In the southwest flow between these features, a shortwave trough was lifting through the Central Plains. These storms formed in the southeast quadrant of the associated vorticity maximum. Around the time thunderstorms developed, temperatures were in the lower 70s with dewpoints in the lower 60s, resulting in low cloud bases. Even at mid-afternoon, cloud bases were only 2000-2500 feet. The surface conditions and mid-level lapse rates of 7 degrees C/km resulted in SBCAPE around 1500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was only 25-30 knots. However, low-level wind fields were strong with 40-45 knots at 850 mb. This resulted in large hodographs with 0-1 km SRH values of 200-250 m2/s2, and 0-3 km SRH of 250-300 m2/s2. Combined with low cloud bases, this created a favorable environment for supercells and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down between Bostwick and Superior, and was rated an EF1 with an estimated peak wind speed of 100 MPH. Trees were damaged, pivots were overturned and power poles were snapped. A home northwest of Superior sustained porch damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the tornado that touched down west-northwest of Webber in Jewell County, KS, traveling northeast through the very northwest corner of Republic County, KS, before entering Nebraska near the town of Hardy in Nuckolls County. It continued traveling northeast into Thayer County, lifting northwest of Deshler. This tornado was rated an EF2, with an estimated peak wind speed of 122 MPH. EF2 damage was noted in both Jewell County, KS and Nuckolls County, NE.||Damage along this tornado path included numerous pivots overturned and snapped power poles. There was a large amount of tree damage noted. In addition, in Thayer County, grain bins and outbuildings were destroyed, and irrigation piping was strewn across a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in central Cherry County and moved southeast producing large hail, heavy rain, and gusty winds. Other thunderstorms developed in the southeast Nebraska Panhandle and southwest that also produced large hail, damaging winds, and heavy rain. The heavy rainfall led to localized flash flooding in portions of Keith, Perkins, Lincoln, and Hayes counties.

Scattered to numerous, semi-discrete and multicell thunderstorms blossomed across much of South Central Nebraska on this Saturday afternoon and evening, with a few storms becoming severe and producing hail ranging from 1-2 in diameter. The first severe storm of the afternoon developed near Kearney around 5 p.m. CDT, and was likely the most prolific hail-producer as it slowly drifted north-northwest through central and northwest Buffalo County for nearly two hours. Just north of Kearney, a few vehicles slid off of Highway 10 as it became hail-covered and slick. This storm also produced the largest reported hail of the day (2) north of Riverdale. Between 6-9 p.m. CDT, several other storms either flared up over various parts of the area or pushed northward out of Kansas, with quarter size hail reported near the state line south of Red Cloud and also in Wood River. Even though the mesoscale environment was potentially supportive of a somewhat greater severe weather threat, the fairly rapid development of plentiful convection and resultant storm-scale outflow interference likely mitigated this. With the loss of diurnal heating, storms steadily weakened and diminished in coverage within a few hours after nightfall. Although most affected areas received less than 1 of rainfall, much of northern Kearney County along with adjacent portions of southeast Buffalo, southwest Hall and northwest Adams counties received higher amounts to around 2. ||In the mid-upper levels, the primary player was an expansive closed low churning slowly eastward through eastern Colorado, with several smaller-scale disturbances shifting northward into the Central Plains along its eastern flank. At the surface, there were no distinct synoptic features within South Central Nebraska, only fairly weak, broad low pressure and prevailing light southerly to easterly breezes. Despite afternoon temperatures only rising into the low-70s F, the combination of surface dewpoints in the low to mid-50s and notably cold air aloft (evidenced by 500 millibar temps around -18 C) yielded surface-based CAPE around 1000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-40 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms led to significant river flooding along many river in southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa during the early part of June. The first episode of heavy rainfall came during the early morning hours on June 4th when thunderstorms lifted north across southeast Nebraska into southwest Iowa to the north of a warm front. Additional thunderstorms moved along this warm front during the morning on the 4th, followed by even heavier and slower moving thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening of the 4th. The combination of these repeated rainfalls on already saturated ground led to river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several heavy rain events in early to mid June brought the the Big Blue River near Crete into flood. The combination of the repeated events allowed for each crest of the river to be a bit higher than the last, finally crested in moderate flood stage at 26.05 feet on June 12th. Besides significant agricultural flooding along both sides of the river, some areas on the east side of Crete near Tuxedo Park on the north side of Highway 33.

Additional thunderstorms moved along this warm front during the morning on the 4th, followed by even heavier and slower moving thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening of the 4th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Repeated episodes of heavy rainfall resulted in a long duration flood along the Big Blue River near Beatrice with multiple crests observed. The initial crest on the 6th of June was the most significant, with a second significant crest on the 17th. Besides significant agricultural flooding along both banks of the river, several rural roads were closed near the river as well as State Highway 136.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two brief tornadoes occurred in Harlan County on this Saturday evening, with a couple reports of severe hail as well nearby. Scattered thunderstorms developed over north central Kansas between 530 and 600 p.m. CST. Several small thunderstorms lifted north, into and through Harlan County, over the following two hours. The last storm to move through produced a couple brief, weak tornadoes around 730 p.m. CST. This storm also produced one inch hail in a couple locations over Phelps County, just after 8 p.m. CST. Other small thunderstorms continued across south central Nebraska, and just before 11 p.m., a storm briefly became strong enough to produce a few one inch hailstones in Kearney. However, most of the hail was the size of nickels or smaller.||A continental polar air mass was over the region, but lee cyclogenesis was underway. By midnight, low pressure had migrated from northwestern Kansas and into south central Nebraska. The associated warm front had also lifted north along with it. Severe thunderstorms developed across northwest Nebraska during the late evening hours on June 20th and continued overnight as storms moved southeast. The storms produced large hail up to the size of softballs and damaging winds.

Thunderstorms developed early this Wednesday afternoon and evening, and predominantly produced tornadoes and flash flooding. With no cap in place, scattered thunderstorms began developing as early as the noon hour over north central Kansas. Most storms rapidly became supercellular. These supercells rapidly lifted north into south central Nebraska and began producing severe weather by 330 p.m. CDT. Most of the tornadoes occurred over Nuckolls and Thayer counties. One tornado did occur over Adams county and a brief spin-up also occurred over Hall county. Eventually, an MCS developed. The leading edge of the cold pool becoming stationary. New storms repeatedly developed over north central Kansas and lifted northeast along the boundary, through Nuckolls and Thayer counties and further downstream into southeastern Nebraska. Cell training produced a swath of 5-11 inches of rain over portions of Nuckolls and Thayer counties. The highest amount reported was 10.91 inches just west of Hebron. This amount of rainfall in 24 hours typically occurs once in 1,000 years at any given location, or stated another way, has a .1% chance of occurrence in a given 24 hour period. Cooperative observers in Hebron and Superior reported 8.59 and 7.13 inches, respectively. These were the highest 24-hour rainfall amounts ever recorded at these locations, dating back to 1893. Flash flooding damaged roads and some bridges were washed out. U.S. Highways 81 and 136 were closed for a time due to the flooding.||A complex low pressure system extended from the Canadian Prairies into the western United States. These storms developed in the warm sector, far away from any discernible surface boundaries. However, an axis of confluence was present over north central Kansas. A deep upper-level trough was over the western U.S., with a ridge over the East. In the southwest flow between these features, a shortwave trough was lifting through the Central Plains. These storms formed in the southeast quadrant of the associated vorticity maximum. Around the time thunderstorms developed, temperatures were in the lower 70s with dewpoints in the lower 60s, resulting in low cloud bases. Even at mid-afternoon, cloud bases were only 2000-2500 feet. The surface conditions and mid-level lapse rates of 7 degrees C/km resulted in SBCAPE around 1500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was only 25-30 knots. However, low-level wind fields were strong with 40-45 knots at 850 mb. This resulted in large hodographs with 0-1 km SRH values of 200-250 m2/s2, and 0-3 km SRH of 250-300 m2/s2. Combined with low cloud bases, this created a favorable environment for supercells and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the tornado that touched down west-northwest of Webber in Jewell County, KS, traveling northeast through the very northwest corner of Republic County, KS, before entering Nebraska near the town of Hardy in Nuckolls County. It continued traveling northeast into Thayer County, lifting northwest of Deshler. This tornado was rated an EF2, with an estimated peak wind speed of 122 MPH. EF2 damage was noted in both Jewell County, KS and Nuckolls County, NE.||Damage along this tornado path included numerous pivots overturned and snapped power poles. There was a large amount of tree damage noted. In addition, in Nuckolls County, grain bins and outbuildings were destroyed. One home sustained chimney and siding damage, while another home lost a large section of its roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the latter half of May, an unusually long-lasting, roughly six-week period of generally minor flooding commenced along the Platte River in South Central Nebraska, impacting local areas from Gothenburg downstream to near Grand Island. This event was spurred by a combination of heavy May rainfall over upstream portions of northeast Colorado and the southern Nebraska Panhandle, coincident with above normal Rocky Mountains snow melt. Although the initial flooding within South Central Nebraska was largely tied to these upstream factors, it then perpetuated through much of June thanks to heavy rain falling directly over the local Platte basin, especially between the Kearney and Grand Island areas. Although peak water levels and impacts during this May-June event closely resembled those which occurred during the Platte River flooding of late-September to early-October 2013, this event was considerably more prolonged. The vast majority of local flooding impacts were noted within Dawson, Buffalo and Hall counties, mainly affecting pastures and lowlands immediately adjacent to the river, with very little impact to infrastructure. That being said, a few locations did experience legitimate disruptions. One of these was the Blue Heron Campground near Gothenburg, and another being the area around the south side of the Interstate 80 exit 312/Highway 281 interchange area near Grand Island, where water occasionally encroached into the parking lots of businesses. Water levels and resultant flooding concerns decreased downstream from the Grand Island area, largely due to the widening of the main river channel. ||Taking a closer look at the onset and timing of Platte River flooding within South Central Nebraska during May, the stage was set by heavy upstream rainfall coinciding with above normal snow melt. For the month of May as a whole, the vast majority of northeast Colorado and the southern Nebraska Panhandle received 5-10 of precipitation, or about 200-400 percent of normal. The Platte started creeping out of its banks in southwest Nebraska during the May 10-15 time frame. Within the local area, minor flooding got underway in the Dawson County area around May 16th, with the automated gauge near Cozad initially cresting around 6.7 feet (flood stage 6.5 feet) on the 20th. Downstream at the gauge near Kearney, flood stage of 6.0 feet was initially exceeded on the 18th, with a pair of crests around 7.0 feet occurring on the 22nd and again on the 31st. Farther east near Grand Island, the river initially crept above the 6.5 ft. flood stage between the 22nd-24th, then briefly dropped below flood for a few days before starting a more persistent rise above flood stage on the 31st. All three of these automated gauges either periodically reached, or consistently stayed above flood stage well into June (please refer to the June Storm Data entry for more specific information regarding the latter stages of this Platte River flooding).

Thunderstorms developed in central Cherry County and moved southeast producing large hail, heavy rain, and gusty winds. Other thunderstorms developed in the southeast Nebraska Panhandle and southwest that also produced large hail, damaging winds, and heavy rain. The heavy rainfall led to localized flash flooding in portions of Keith, Perkins, Lincoln, and Hayes counties.

Despite marginal convective instability, the first severe thunderstorms of 2015 rumbled across mainly the eastern two-thirds of South Central Nebraska on this Wednesday evening, resulting in several reports of mostly marginally severe hail and wind gusts along an advancing cold front. Nearly all thunderstorms focused east of a Greeley-Kearney-Alma line, and assumed primarily a quasi-linear, multicell mode. In terms of hail, most was only up to quarter size, including several reports from the vicinities of Boelus, Ravenna, Alma, Doniphan, Trumbull and Deshler. The largest reported stones of half dollar size fell in Waco. As for wind gusts, speeds up to around 60 MPH were reported in or near Franklin, Blue Hill, Edgar and Ayr, with a home weather station northwest of Red Cloud clocking a peak gust of 73 MPH. Although no tornadoes occurred, the environment was conducive for a few gustnadoes along thunderstorm outflow, including one captured on storm chaser video east of Alma. On another severe weather side note, an intense lightning bolt struck a large tree in Hastings around 915 p.m. CDT, setting the tree on fire and scattering limbs as far as 100 feet. Rainfall-wise, the vast majority of the local area received well under 1, with the main exception of higher amounts concentrated in Nuckolls and Thayer counties, where a NeRAIN observer near Nora measured 1.77. ||Breaking down event timing, storms first flared up along the well-defined surface cold front between 6-7 p.m. CDT, with convection then expanding in coverage and tracking east through the evening. Although most strong to severe storms exited east of the local area by 10 p.m. CDT, regenerative development of stronger cores along the southern end of the complex persisted through around midnight CDT, mainly in Nuckolls/Thayer counties. All in all, this was a fairly standard issue first severe weather event of the season, primarily driven by low-level frontal forcing. Early in the evening, this northeast-southwest oriented front stretched across the heart of the local area, tied into a 998 millibar low pressure center in north central Kansas. Although afternoon temperatures ahead of the boundary warmed well into the 80s F, rather meager dewpoints only in the 40s likely precluded a potentially greater severe weather threat. Nonetheless, the presence of steep low-mid level lapse rates promoted sufficient mixed-layer CAPE of 500-1000 J/kg in the presence of decent 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-50 knots. In the mid-upper levels, southwesterly flow was fairly modest for this time of year, as the primary large scale trough shifted from the Northern Rockies into the Northern Plains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind knocked down a portion of a wood fence and small tree debris was blown across the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite marginal convective instability, the first severe thunderstorms of 2015 rumbled across mainly the eastern two-thirds of South Central Nebraska on this Wednesday evening, resulting in several reports of mostly marginally severe hail and wind gusts along an advancing cold front. Nearly all thunderstorms focused east of a Greeley-Kearney-Alma line, and assumed primarily a quasi-linear, multicell mode. In terms of hail, most was only up to quarter size, including several reports from the vicinities of Boelus, Ravenna, Alma, Doniphan, Trumbull and Deshler. The largest reported stones of half dollar size fell in Waco. As for wind gusts, speeds up to around 60 MPH were reported in or near Franklin, Blue Hill, Edgar and Ayr, with a home weather station northwest of Red Cloud clocking a peak gust of 73 MPH. Although no tornadoes occurred, the environment was conducive for a few gustnadoes along thunderstorm outflow, including one captured on storm chaser video east of Alma. On another severe weather side note, an intense lightning bolt struck a large tree in Hastings around 915 p.m. CDT, setting the tree on fire and scattering limbs as far as 100 feet. Rainfall-wise, the vast majority of the local area received well under 1, with the main exception of higher amounts concentrated in Nuckolls and Thayer counties, where a NeRAIN observer near Nora measured 1.77. ||Breaking down event timing, storms first flared up along the well-defined surface cold front between 6-7 p.m. CDT, with convection then expanding in coverage and tracking east through the evening. Although most strong to severe storms exited east of the local area by 10 p.m. CDT, regenerative development of stronger cores along the southern end of the complex persisted through around midnight CDT, mainly in Nuckolls/Thayer counties. All in all, this was a fairly standard issue first severe weather event of the season, primarily driven by low-level frontal forcing. Early in the evening, this northeast-southwest oriented front stretched across the heart of the local area, tied into a 998 millibar low pressure center in north central Kansas. Although afternoon temperatures ahead of the boundary warmed well into the 80s F, rather meager dewpoints only in the 40s likely precluded a potentially greater severe weather threat. Nonetheless, the presence of steep low-mid level lapse rates promoted sufficient mixed-layer CAPE of 500-1000 J/kg in the presence of decent 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-50 knots. In the mid-upper levels, southwesterly flow was fairly modest for this time of year, as the primary large scale trough shifted from the Northern Rockies into the Northern Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was measured at a home weather station.

A couple storms briefly produced sub-severe hail on this Friday evening and night. Just after 7 p.m. CST, a string of isolated showers, and a couple embedded thunderstorms, developed along the Highway 136 corridor. Around 9 pm, the storm on the western end of the string strengthened over northwestern Franklin county. This storm maintained its strength as it moved east-northeast, and over southwestern Clay county, it dropped hail up to the size of dimes. The storm then weakened over Fillmore county around midnight. Shortly thereafter, a new line of scattered thunderstorms formed from Fillmore county northwest across Clay, Hall, and Buffalo counties. As this line drifted east, one of the embedded storms briefly intensified, producing hail up to the size of nickels near Grand Island.||Late in the day on Thursday, April 30th, a very weak Canadian cold front moved into Nebraska. This front became stationary across the central third of Nebraska Friday and eventually dissipated by evening. There was very little forcing for these storms aloft. Seasonably strong high pressure was over the Desert Southwest, with zonal flow from the Pacific Northwest into the Northern Plains. All this convective activity formed in an area of low-level convergence, near the frontal remnants, at the terminus of a 35 knot low-level jet. Mid-level lapse rates and instability were low. MUCAPE was around 500 J/kg. Shear in the cloud-bearing layer was 20 to 30 knots. However, this environment was sufficient to produce storms strong enough for small hail.

Two brief tornadoes occurred in Harlan County on this Saturday evening, with a couple reports of severe hail as well nearby. Scattered thunderstorms developed over north central Kansas between 530 and 600 p.m. CST. Several small thunderstorms lifted north, into and through Harlan County, over the following two hours. The last storm to move through produced a couple brief, weak tornadoes around 730 p.m. CST. This storm also produced one inch hail in a couple locations over Phelps County, just after 8 p.m. CST. Other small thunderstorms continued across south central Nebraska, and just before 11 p.m., a storm briefly became strong enough to produce a few one inch hailstones in Kearney. However, most of the hail was the size of nickels or smaller.||A continental polar air mass was over the region, but lee cyclogenesis was underway. By midnight, low pressure had migrated from northwestern Kansas and into south central Nebraska. The associated warm front had also lifted north along with it. At upper-levels, a deep trough was over the Rockies with a closed low over Colorado, and a coupled jet streak configuration. A ridge was downstream over the eastern United States. South central Nebraska had been shrouded in low stratus and fog all day, with temperatures struggling to rise through the 50s. Dewpoints depressions were very low. MUCAPE was only 500 J/kg, but deep layer shear was around 45 knots. Strengthening and significantly veering low-level wind fields resulted in 0-1 km SRH of 200-300 m2/s2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a brief touchdown north-northeast of Stamford. No damage was reported with this tornado.

Thunderstorms developed early this Wednesday afternoon and evening, and predominantly produced tornadoes and flash flooding. With no cap in place, scattered thunderstorms began developing as early as the noon hour over north central Kansas. Most storms rapidly became supercellular. These supercells rapidly lifted north into south central Nebraska and began producing severe weather by 330 p.m. CDT. Most of the tornadoes occurred over Nuckolls and Thayer counties. One tornado did occur over Adams county and a brief spin-up also occurred over Hall county. Eventually, an MCS developed. The leading edge of the cold pool becoming stationary. New storms repeatedly developed over north central Kansas and lifted northeast along the boundary, through Nuckolls and Thayer counties and further downstream into southeastern Nebraska. Cell training produced a swath of 5-11 inches of rain over portions of Nuckolls and Thayer counties. The highest amount reported was 10.91 inches just west of Hebron. This amount of rainfall in 24 hours typically occurs once in 1,000 years at any given location, or stated another way, has a .1% chance of occurrence in a given 24 hour period. Cooperative observers in Hebron and Superior reported 8.59 and 7.13 inches, respectively. These were the highest 24-hour rainfall amounts ever recorded at these locations, dating back to 1893. Flash flooding damaged roads and some bridges were washed out. U.S. Highways 81 and 136 were closed for a time due to the flooding.||A complex low pressure system extended from the Canadian Prairies into the western United States. These storms developed in the warm sector, far away from any discernible surface boundaries. However, an axis of confluence was present over north central Kansas. A deep upper-level trough was over the western U.S., with a ridge over the East. In the southwest flow between these features, a shortwave trough was lifting through the Central Plains. These storms formed in the southeast quadrant of the associated vorticity maximum. Around the time thunderstorms developed, temperatures were in the lower 70s with dewpoints in the lower 60s, resulting in low cloud bases. Even at mid-afternoon, cloud bases were only 2000-2500 feet. The surface conditions and mid-level lapse rates of 7 degrees C/km resulted in SBCAPE around 1500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was only 25-30 knots. However, low-level wind fields were strong with 40-45 knots at 850 mb. This resulted in large hodographs with 0-1 km SRH values of 200-250 m2/s2, and 0-3 km SRH of 250-300 m2/s2. Combined with low cloud bases, this created a favorable environment for supercells and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the tornado that touched down in the Scandia area of Republic County, KS.  This tornado was rated an EF2, with an estimated peak wind speed of 120 MPH. The strongest and widest portion of this tornado occurred in Republic County, KS.||In the Thayer County, NE, portion of this tornado, trees were damaged and pivots were overturned. Power poles and grain bins were damaged. One home southeast of Hebron had its roof partially removed. This tornado crossed Highway 81 approximately 6 miles south of Hebron.

A deep upper-level trough was over the western U.S., with a ridge over the East. In the southwest flow between these features, a shortwave trough was lifting through the Central Plains. These storms formed in the southeast quadrant of the associated vorticity maximum. Around the time thunderstorms developed, temperatures were in the lower 70s with dewpoints in the lower 60s, resulting in low cloud bases. Even at mid-afternoon, cloud bases were only 2000-2500 feet. The surface conditions and mid-level lapse rates of 7 degrees C/km resulted in SBCAPE around 1500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was only 25-30 knots. However, low-level wind fields were strong with 40-45 knots at 850 mb. This resulted in large hodographs with 0-1 km SRH values of 200-250 m2/s2, and 0-3 km SRH of 250-300 m2/s2. Combined with low cloud bases, this created a favorable environment for supercells and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was rated an EF0, with an estimated peak wind speed of 80 MPH. Damage from this tornado included a few overturned train cars west of Carleton and overturned irrigation pivots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early afternoon thunderstorms over Morrill County produced large hail, damaging winds and very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed 16 miles east-northeast of Lynn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early afternoon thunderstorms over Morrill County produced large hail, damaging winds and very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rain from thunderstorms produced flash flooding.  Highway 92 was closed by high water from County Road 119 to the intersection of Highway 385 west of Broadwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds were estimated near 60 mph over part of Harlan County on this Saturday evening. Areas of leftover rain, mainly from earlier thunderstorms, were lifting north out of Kansas. Along the leading edge, an embedded thunderstorm developed over Harlan County. Winds were estimated near 60 mph just northwest of Huntley. Radar suggested that these winds could have been associated with an outflow boundary associated with the earlier thunderstorms.||An occluding low pressure system was over the Northern Plains. Its associated cold front extended southward into western Nebraska. A deep trough was over the western United States, and a ridge over the East. A negatively-tilted shortwave trough was exiting out of the trough base into the Southern Plains. MUCAPE was only 500 J/kg. However, deep layer shear was around 50 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms moved into the southeastern panhandle during the evening on June 1st.  Damaging winds of 60 mph caused tree damage near Chappell in Deuel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts downed 6 to 8 inch diameter tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across northwest Nebraska during the late evening hours on June 20th and continued overnight as storms moved southeast. The storms produced large hail up to the size of softballs and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General public located 5 miles southwest of White Clay estimated thunderstorm wind gusts of 70 mph for a few minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across northwest Nebraska during the late evening hours on June 20th and continued overnight as storms moved southeast. The storms produced large hail up to the size of softballs and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located 3 miles north northwest of Rushville reported several trees were blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed early this Wednesday afternoon and evening, and predominantly produced tornadoes and flash flooding. With no cap in place, scattered thunderstorms began developing as early as the noon hour over north central Kansas. Most storms rapidly became supercellular. These supercells rapidly lifted north into south central Nebraska and began producing severe weather by 330 p.m. CDT. Most of the tornadoes occurred over Nuckolls and Thayer counties. One tornado did occur over Adams county and a brief spin-up also occurred over Hall county. Eventually, an MCS developed. The leading edge of the cold pool becoming stationary. New storms repeatedly developed over north central Kansas and lifted northeast along the boundary, through Nuckolls and Thayer counties and further downstream into southeastern Nebraska. Cell training produced a swath of 5-11 inches of rain over portions of Nuckolls and Thayer counties. The highest amount reported was 10.91 inches just west of Hebron. This amount of rainfall in 24 hours typically occurs once in 1,000 years at any given location, or stated another way, has a .1% chance of occurrence in a given 24 hour period. Cooperative observers in Hebron and Superior reported 8.59 and 7.13 inches, respectively. These were the highest 24-hour rainfall amounts ever recorded at these locations, dating back to 1893. Flash flooding damaged roads and some bridges were washed out. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon and evening thunderstorms produced very heavy rain, strong winds and numerous reports of large hail across central and southern portions of the western Nebraska Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly two inches of rain in 30 to 45 minutes resulted in widespread flash flooding in and around Scottsbluff.  There were numerous reports of more than a foot of water running over streets in Scottsbluff.  Several businesses were flooded on Broadway Street.  Vehicles were stranded in flood waters on 5th Avenue in Scottsbluff.  County Road 15 was closed west of  Scottsbluff.  Standing water was reported on Highways 26 and 92.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon and evening thunderstorms produced very heavy rain, strong winds and numerous reports of large hail across central and southern portions of the western Nebraska Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly two inches of rain in 30 to 45 minutes resulted in flash flooding.  Portions of County Road 15 and trees were washed away a mile east of Mitchell.  County Road 15 was closed between Mitchell and Scottsbluff.  Standing water was reported on Highway 92 between Scottsbluff and Mitchell.

An isolated storm produced hail up to the size of quarters on this Friday evening. Early in the evening, a few isolated, small storms formed over north central Kansas and lifted north into south central Nebraska, west of Highway 183. The storm that tracked through Furnas and Gosper counties produced hail up to the size of quarters around mid-evening, west and north of Arapahoe.||These storms formed in the warm sector of occluding low pressure over Wyoming. A longwave trough was over the western United States, with a ridge over the East, resulting in southwest flow over the Plains. What exactly ignited these storms was unclear, as synoptic and mesoscale forcing were weak. MLCAPE was 1000-1500 J/kg and deep layer shear was 40 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple storms briefly produced sub-severe hail on this Friday evening and night. Just after 7 p.m. CST, a string of isolated showers, and a couple embedded thunderstorms, developed along the Highway 136 corridor. Around 9 pm, the storm on the western end of the string strengthened over northwestern Franklin county. This storm maintained its strength as it moved east-northeast, and over southwestern Clay county, it dropped hail up to the size of dimes. The storm then weakened over Fillmore county around midnight. Shortly thereafter, a new line of scattered thunderstorms formed from Fillmore county northwest across Clay, Hall, and Buffalo counties. As this line drifted east, one of the embedded storms briefly intensified, producing hail up to the size of nickels near Grand Island.||Late in the day on Thursday, April 30th, a very weak Canadian cold front moved into Nebraska. This front became stationary across the central third of Nebraska Friday and eventually dissipated by evening. There was very little forcing for these storms aloft. Seasonably strong high pressure was over the Desert Southwest, with zonal flow from the Pacific Northwest into the Northern Plains. All this convective activity formed in an area of low-level convergence, near the frontal remnants, at the terminus of a 35 knot low-level jet. Mid-level lapse rates and instability were low. MUCAPE was around 500 J/kg. Shear in the cloud-bearing layer was 20 to 30 knots. However, this environment was sufficient to produce storms strong enough for small hail.
Afternoon thunderstorms produced large hail, strong winds and torrential rain across portions of the western Nebraska Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road 13 was flooded with two to three feet of water west of Scottsbluff.  Several other roads near the Mitchell and Gering canals were flooded.  Highway 92 was flooded at two locations with swift flowing water six to eight inches deep.  County Road L between County Road 16 and Rifle Site Road was covered by fast moving water.  South Mitchell Road flooded from just south of Mitchell to Highway 92.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe hail and winds occurred in Furnas county late this Friday afternoon. Scattered thunderstorms developed over southwestern Nebraska during the early and mid afternoon hours. By 4 p.m. CST, several storms began began affecting northern and western Furnas county, producing hail up to one inch in diameter and estimated winds up to 60 mph. By 5 p.m., these storms had combined into a very small bow echo configuration over the eastern part of the county. This bow echo then raced across north central Kansas producing a swath of wind damage.||These storms formed within a cold frontal zone. Aloft, a trough was over the Rockies with an embedded shortwave trough advancing through the Northern and Central Plains. A ridge was over the eastern United States. Temperatures in the upper 70s and dewpoints in the upper 50s combined with poor mid-level lapse rates to produce MLCAPE between 500 and 1000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 35-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH.

Isolated thunderstorms also occurred in Blaine County of north central Nebraska during the early evening hours producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to ping pong ball size with 40 to 50 mph wind gusts broke windows on north side of house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced a landspout tornado late morning near North Platte, then damaging winds by early evening near North Platte.  In Hooker County, large hail was reported in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1125CST, a landspout tornado formed about 7 miles south southeast of North Platte in an open field and drifted slowly to the west. The tornado lifted at 1129CST with no damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms led to significant river flooding along many river in southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa during the early part of June. The first episode of heavy rainfall came during the early morning hours on June 4th when thunderstorms lifted north across southeast Nebraska into southwest Iowa to the north of a warm front. Additional thunderstorms moved along this warm front during the morning on the 4th, followed by even heavier and slower moving thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening of the 4th. The combination of these repeated rainfalls on already saturated ground led to river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was observed along the Missouri River near Rulo. The river crested 3.34 feet above flood stage. The flooding was confined to mainly low areas near the river channel.

Quarter size hail was reported in two locations on this Saturday evening. Late in the afternoon, scattered thunderstorms developed between Ord and McCook. The storms were brief and quickly dissipated along and south of Interstate 80. To the north of the Interstate, instability was greater. The storms continued and strengthened, with a couple briefly turning severe, producing hail up to 1 inch in diameter near Greeley and Clarks. ||These storms formed within a broad warm sector, ahead of the lee-side trough that became mobile and advanced east into the central third of Nebraska. There was no discernible forcing aloft as the flow was zonal, and the subtropical and polar jet streams were far to the south and north, respectively. Surface dewpoint depressions were large, with temperatures in the mid 80s and dewpoints in the upper 40s. However, mid-level lapse rates were around 7.5 deg C/km, resulting in MLCAPE of 1000-1250 J/kg north of I-80. Deep layer shear was 35-40 knots.

During the late afternoon and evening hours on this Thursday, scattered to numerous thunderstorms rumbled through South Central Nebraska counties mainly along and south of the Interstate 80 corridor, resulting in a handful of sub-severe hail reports in the penny to nickel size range. Although radar data suggested that various other locations likely experienced hail up to around this size, the only ground-truth reports originated from Hastings, Edgar and from the vicinities of Kearney, Sutton and Clay Center. While most of this convection was multicellular and linear, there were a few briefly semi-discrete structures as well. Rainfall amounts under most of the heaviest convection averaged between 0.50 and 1, with only few localized higher exceptions. ||Strong storms first developed into southwestern counties such as Harlan and Franklin between 430-600 p.m. CDT. Then between 6-9 p.m. CDT, a couple of narrow west-to-east arcs of strong storms developed eastward through the Highway 6 and Interstate 80 corridors and lifted slowly north, resulting in the aforementioned hail reports. After nightfall, a few stronger storms lingered over mainly Thayer and Fillmore counties for a time, but these steadily faded with the loss of diurnal heating. In the mid and upper levels, weak forcing was provided by small-scale disturbances shifting into the Central Plains on southerly flow aloft, well-downstream from an expansive large-scale closed low spinning over the Four Corners region. At the surface, afternoon high temperatures only climbed between the mid-60s and low-70s F across most of the area, but the combination of surface dewpoints in the mid-50s and rather cold air aloft (evidenced by 500 millibar temps around -16 C) was sufficient to promote modest surface-based CAPE as high as 500-1000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear only around 30 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail occurred on the east side of Edgar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms led to significant river flooding along many river in southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa during the early part of June. The first episode of heavy rainfall came during the early morning hours on June 4th when thunderstorms lifted north across southeast Nebraska into southwest Iowa to the north of a warm front. Additional thunderstorms moved along this warm front during the morning on the 4th, followed by even heavier and slower moving thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening of the 4th. The combination of these repeated rainfalls on already saturated ground led to river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding was observed along the Big Nemaha River near Falls City. The flooding including closing of many gravel and county roads, as well State Highway 8. The flooding also included agricultural low-lands along the river as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred along the West Fork of Big Blue River, especially upstream of Dorchester near Beaver Crossing. Only minor low-land agricultural flooding near the river channel was observed near Dorchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front, coupled with an upper level trough of low pressure, produced widespread severe thunderstorms across the northeastern Nebraska Panhandle and north central Nebraska on during the late afternoon and evening of July 5th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the third consecutive night, this time from the evening of Thursday the 4th into the early morning of Friday the 5th, several rounds of thunderstorms rumbled across much of the 24-county South Central Nebraska area. And for the second straight night, the majority of storms concentrated within the southeastern half of the area. Although the first few hours of evening convection yielded several hail reports up to mainly quarter size within Hall, Hamilton, Merrick and York counties, by far the biggest story of the night, and for some places for the next several days thereafter, was heavy rainfall and resultant flooding. During the 24-hour period ending around sunrise on the 5th, widespread rainfall of 2-4 drenched several counties, primarily including: Adams, Clay, Fillmore, York, Nuckolls, Thayer, southern Hall and southern Hamilton. Combined with as much as 1.50-3.00 that fell across some of these same areas during the preceding night, the net result was 36-hour rain totals of generally 3-7, with the overall-highest concentration of 5-7 impacting a fairly narrow, roughly 10-mile wide corridor extending through southeast Hall, northeast Adams, southern Hamilton, northern Clay and western Fillmore counties. As just a few specific examples of 36-hour measured totals ending around sunrise on the 5th: 6.44 at Harvard and 5.28 four miles west of Geneva (NeRAIN observers). For the calendar days of June 3rd-5th, Hastings airport totaled 5.39, of which 4.74 fell on the 4th. This total set a new Hastings daily record for June rainfall, and was the third-highest daily total for any day of the year. Needless to say, widespread flooding of creeks, streams and primarily rural roadways was well-underway in the aforementioned counties by daybreak on the 5th. Adams county Emergency Management issued a press release alerting citizens that some roads are impassable and dangerous due to standing water and possible unknown damage in the northeast part of the county. Earlier on, during the initial stages of heavy rain on the evening of the 4th, significant street flooding impacted many communities including Grand Island, where several city streets were closed for a few hours. Also near Grand Island on the evening of the 4th, the Platte River (which had already been slightly above flood stage for a few weeks) briefly rose far enough out of its banks to overflow Locust Street near the Interstate 80 exit 314 interchange. Getting back to the daytime hours on the 5th, although flooding of roads and small streams began to very slowly subside, issues began ramping up on larger creeks and rivers. Most notably, this included the West Fork Big Blue River, which was centered almost directly under the narrow axis of heaviest rain and experienced historic flooding over the next several days (please refer to the separate paragraph below for more details regarding West Fork Big Blue River flooding).||Taking a closer look at the timing and evolution of local thunderstorm activity from the afternoon of the 4th into the early morning of the 5th...there were several different rounds. Following a brief lull in the wake of widespread activity during the early morning of the 4th, the next round of mainly non-severe storms affected counties primarily along/north of Interstate 80 through most of the afternoon hours. These storms yielded only one severe weather report: quarter size hail near Pleasanton. Following another brief lull during the late afternoon and early evening, a narrow swath of hail and torrential rain-producing severe storms blew up between 7-9 p.m. CDT along an axis from near Grand Island and Central City, eastward to near Stromsburg and York. Between 9-11 p.m. CDT, this vigorous line of convection gradually weakened as it sank southward toward the Kansas border, but continued dumping heavy rain. Beyond 11 p.m., the next several hours featured widespread, persistent development of non-severe-but-heavy-rain producing convection over most counties along and south of Interstate 80, largely driven by convergence/lift along the nose of a nocturnal, 30-40 knot low level jet. Finally, between 3-6 a.m. CDT on the 5th, storms steadily diminished in intensity and ended from north-to-south, leaving only lingering light rain over the Nebraska-Kansas border area by sunrise. Turning to the synoptic and mesoscale setup: this was a fairly similar situation to the night before, as a quasi-stationary composite surface front/outflow boundary remained in place near the Nebraska-Kansas border. Although the primary large scale mid-upper trough remained well to the west along the California coast, a series of downstream low-amplitude disturbances passed through the Central Plains in quasi-zonal flow, interacting with an unstable and very moist airmass to trigger vigorous convection. Around the time of the initial evening hail-producing storms, the mesoscale environment featured around 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 30-40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep-layer wind shear. ||June 5th-12th Summary of Historic Flooding Along the West Fork Big Blue River from near the Adams/Clay County line downstream through southeastern York County...deemed to be the worst flooding in 50-80 years: As detailed in the first paragraph above, 36-hour rainfall totals averaged 5-7 along much of the local stretch of the West Fork Big Blue River between the evening of June 3rd and the morning of June 5th. As a result, significant flooding commenced along the river and its smaller tributaries on the 5th, with the primary flood crest initially working its way across the northern Clay and southern Hamilton County area. By the daylight hours on Saturday the 6th, the seriousness of the flooding situation became more apparent for downstream communities such as McCool Junction. Residents in southern Hamilton County were already calling it the worst flooding they had witnessed in 50-80 years, as the crest reached the Lushton area of York County by late morning. Around this time, officials in McCool Junction began evacuations of several homes on the east side of town along the rising river, along with sandbagging operations. Around 12:30 p.m. CDT, water entered a church camp just south of town, cutting off access to it. The West Fork Big Blue crested in the immediate McCool Junction area that evening, but only a few homes suffered significant flooding. It was reported that at least five homes were saved due to advance notice of the approaching crest and resultant sandbagging. By daylight on Sunday morning June 7th, water levels had receded 1-2 feet at McCool Junction, as the crest worked downstream to near the York/Seward County line. Over the next few days, a welcomed-stretch of warm and dry weather allowed the river to slowly recede back within its banks over southern York County, and the initial multi-day Flood Warning was allowed to expire on Wednesday, June 10th. However, Mother Nature soon threw one last curveball at the McCool Junction area, as yet another round of heavy rain on the order of 1.50-3.00 fell into much of the West Fork Big Blue basin on the evening of the 10th into the morning of Thursday the 11th. As a result, a secondary, less-severe round of flooding was reported at McCool Junction by 10 a.m. CDT on the 11th, with the river reported to be one foot out of its banks. This second round of primarily minor flooding persisted into the daytime hours on Friday, June 12th, but largely abated by sunset that evening, finally ending over one week of flooding issues along the West Fork Big Blue River in eastern portions of South Central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mostly nickel size hail fell, with a few quarter size hail stones mixed in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the third consecutive night, this time from the evening of Thursday the 4th into the early morning of Friday the 5th, several rounds of thunderstorms rumbled across much of the 24-county South Central Nebraska area. And for the second straight night, the majority of storms concentrated within the southeastern half of the area. Although the first few hours of evening convection yielded several hail reports up to mainly quarter size within Hall, Hamilton, Merrick and York counties, by far the biggest story of the night, and for some places for the next several days thereafter, was heavy rainfall and resultant flooding. During the 24-hour period ending around sunrise on the 5th, widespread rainfall of 2-4 drenched several counties, primarily including: Adams, Clay, Fillmore, York, Nuckolls, Thayer, southern Hall and southern Hamilton. Combined with as much as 1.50-3.00 that fell across some of these same areas during the preceding night, the net result was 36-hour rain totals of generally 3-7, with the overall-highest concentration of 5-7 impacting a fairly narrow, roughly 10-mile wide corridor extending through southeast Hall, northeast Adams, southern Hamilton, northern Clay and western Fillmore counties. As just a few specific examples of 36-hour measured totals ending around sunrise on the 5th: 6.44 at Harvard and 5.28 four miles west of Geneva (NeRAIN observers). For the calendar days of June 3rd-5th, Hastings airport totaled 5.39, of which 4.74 fell on the 4th. This total set a new Hastings daily record for June rainfall, and was the third-highest daily total for any day of the year. Needless to say, widespread flooding of creeks, streams and primarily rural roadways was well-underway in the aforementioned counties by daybreak on the 5th. Adams county Emergency Management issued a press release alerting citizens that some roads are impassable and dangerous due to standing water and possible unknown damage in the northeast part of the county. Earlier on, during the initial stages of heavy rain on the evening of the 4th, significant street flooding impacted many communities including Grand Island, where several city streets were closed for a few hours. Also near Grand Island on the evening of the 4th, the Platte River (which had already been slightly above flood stage for a few weeks) briefly rose far enough out of its banks to overflow Locust Street near the Interstate 80 exit 314 interchange. Getting back to the daytime hours on the 5th, although flooding of roads and small streams began to very slowly subside, issues began ramping up on larger creeks and rivers. Most notably, this included the West Fork Big Blue River, which was centered almost directly under the narrow axis of heaviest rain and experienced historic flooding over the next several days (please refer to the separate paragraph below for more details regarding West Fork Big Blue River flooding).||Taking a closer look at the timing and evolution of local thunderstorm activity from the afternoon of the 4th into the early morning of the 5th...there were several different rounds. Following a brief lull in the wake of widespread activity during the early morning of the 4th, the next round of mainly non-severe storms affected counties primarily along/north of Interstate 80 through most of the afternoon hours. These storms yielded only one severe weather report: quarter size hail near Pleasanton. Following another brief lull during the late afternoon and early evening, a narrow swath of hail and torrential rain-producing severe storms blew up between 7-9 p.m. CDT along an axis from near Grand Island and Central City, eastward to near Stromsburg and York. Between 9-11 p.m. CDT, this vigorous line of convection gradually weakened as it sank southward toward the Kansas border, but continued dumping heavy rain. Beyond 11 p.m., the next several hours featured widespread, persistent development of non-severe-but-heavy-rain producing convection over most counties along and south of Interstate 80, largely driven by convergence/lift along the nose of a nocturnal, 30-40 knot low level jet. Finally, between 3-6 a.m. CDT on the 5th, storms steadily diminished in intensity and ended from north-to-south, leaving only lingering light rain over the Nebraska-Kansas border area by sunrise. Turning to the synoptic and mesoscale setup: this was a fairly similar situation to the night before, as a quasi-stationary composite surface front/outflow boundary remained in place near the Nebraska-Kansas border. Although the primary large scale mid-upper trough remained well to the west along the California coast, a series of downstream low-amplitude disturbances passed through the Central Plains in quasi-zonal flow, interacting with an unstable and very moist airmass to trigger vigorous convection. Around the time of the initial evening hail-producing storms, the mesoscale environment featured around 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 30-40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep-layer wind shear. ||June 5th-12th Summary of Historic Flooding Along the West Fork Big Blue River from near the Adams/Clay County line downstream through southeastern York County...deemed to be the worst flooding in 50-80 years: As detailed in the first paragraph above, 36-hour rainfall totals averaged 5-7 along much of the local stretch of the West Fork Big Blue River between the evening of June 3rd and the morning of June 5th. As a result, significant flooding commenced along the river and its smaller tributaries on the 5th, with the primary flood crest initially working its way across the northern Clay and southern Hamilton County area. By the daylight hours on Saturday the 6th, the seriousness of the flooding situation became more apparent for downstream communities such as McCool Junction. Residents in southern Hamilton County were already calling it the worst flooding they had witnessed in 50-80 years, as the crest reached the Lushton area of York County by late morning. Around this time, officials in McCool Junction began evacuations of several homes on the east side of town along the rising river, along with sandbagging operations. Around 12:30 p.m. CDT, water entered a church camp just south of town, cutting off access to it. The West Fork Big Blue crested in the immediate McCool Junction area that evening, but only a few homes suffered significant flooding. It was reported that at least five homes were saved due to advance notice of the approaching crest and resultant sandbagging. By daylight on Sunday morning June 7th, water levels had receded 1-2 feet at McCool Junction, as the crest worked downstream to near the York/Seward County line. Over the next few days, a welcomed-stretch of warm and dry weather allowed the river to slowly recede back within its banks over southern York County, and the initial multi-day Flood Warning was allowed to expire on Wednesday, June 10th. However, Mother Nature soon threw one last curveball at the McCool Junction area, as yet another round of heavy rain on the order of 1.50-3.00 fell into much of the West Fork Big Blue basin on the evening of the 10th into the morning of Thursday the 11th. As a result, a secondary, less-severe round of flooding was reported at McCool Junction by 10 a.m. CDT on the 11th, with the river reported to be one foot out of its banks. This second round of primarily minor flooding persisted into the daytime hours on Friday, June 12th, but largely abated by sunset that evening, finally ending over one week of flooding issues along the West Fork Big Blue River in eastern portions of South Central Nebraska.

Around the time of the initial evening hail-producing storms, the mesoscale environment featured around 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 30-40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep-layer wind shear. ||June 5th-12th Summary of Historic Flooding Along the West Fork Big Blue River from near the Adams/Clay County line downstream through southeastern York County...deemed to be the worst flooding in 50-80 years: As detailed in the first paragraph above, 36-hour rainfall totals averaged 5-7 along much of the local stretch of the West Fork Big Blue River between the evening of June 3rd and the morning of June 5th. As a result, significant flooding commenced along the river and its smaller tributaries on the 5th, with the primary flood crest initially working its way across the northern Clay and southern Hamilton County area. By the daylight hours on Saturday the 6th, the seriousness of the flooding situation became more apparent for downstream communities such as McCool Junction. Residents in southern Hamilton County were already calling it the worst flooding they had witnessed in 50-80 years, as the crest reached the Lushton area of York County by late morning. Around this time, officials in McCool Junction began evacuations of several homes on the east side of town along the rising river, along with sandbagging operations. Around 12:30 p.m. CDT, water entered a church camp just south of town, cutting off access to it. The West Fork Big Blue crested in the immediate McCool Junction area that evening, but only a few homes suffered significant flooding. It was reported that at least five homes were saved due to advance notice of the approaching crest and resultant sandbagging. By daylight on Sunday morning June 7th, water levels had receded 1-2 feet at McCool Junction, as the crest worked downstream to near the York/Seward County line. Over the next few days, a welcomed-stretch of warm and dry weather allowed the river to slowly recede back within its banks over southern York County, and the initial multi-day Flood Warning was allowed to expire on Wednesday, June 10th. However, Mother Nature soon threw one last curveball at the McCool Junction area, as yet another round of heavy rain on the order of 1.50-3.00 fell into much of the West Fork Big Blue basin on the evening of the 10th into the morning of Thursday the 11th. As a result, a secondary, less-severe round of flooding was reported at McCool Junction by 10 a.m. CDT on the 11th, with the river reported to be one foot out of its banks. This second round of primarily minor flooding persisted into the daytime hours on Friday, June 12th, but largely abated by sunset that evening, finally ending over one week of flooding issues along the West Fork Big Blue River in eastern portions of South Central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24-hour rainfall total of 3.30...most of which fell during the evening of the 4th into the early morning of the 5th. Two-day rainfall totals of 3-7 were common within much of Adams, Clay, Fillmore, Nuckolls, Thayer, southern Hall, southern Hamilton and southern York counties, resulting in significant flooding across parts of the area.

Numerous strong to occasionally severe multicell storms blossomed across South Central Nebraska from the late evening of Wednesday the 3rd into the morning hours of Thursday the 4th, serving as round 1 of an active few days of severe storms and flooding rainfall. With storms ranging in character from semi-discrete to clusters and line segments, they primarily produced hail up to around quarter size, along with heavy rain. Although convection affected parts of several counties at some point during the night, the majority of ground-truth severe weather reports emanated from Adams and Clay counties, including quarter size hail on the east side of Hastings, hail up to golf ball size in Clay Center, and several hours later, a combination of quarter size hail and estimated 60 MPH winds six miles east-southeast of Clay Center. Rainfall-wise, the big story of the night was a concentrated swath of generally 1.50-3.00 that fell over southern Hall/northern Adams counties, southeastward through most of Clay, Fillmore and northern Thayer counties. Within this corridor, a few of the highest overnight totals from NeRAIN observers included 3.13 near Alexandria and 3.00 a few miles northwest of Hastings. As a result, minor flooding affected several creeks and streams and some roadways. In fact, the southbound lanes of Highway 281 in far-southern Hall County were closed for a few hours during the night due to flooding. Although this generally minor flooding subsided during the day of the 4th, unfortunately many of these same areas would soon be drenched with another 2-4 on the evening of the 4th into the morning of the 5th, resulting in 36-hour totals of 3-7 and a more serious, widespread flooding situation (please refer to the separate June 4th p.m.-June 5th a.m. severe weather episode for further details). ||Breaking down the overall event timing, all convection through the pre-dark hours focused to the south over northern Kansas. Then, between 10 p.m.-midnight CDT, storms began developing northward into the southern two rows of South Central Nebraska counties, largely in response to a strengthening, northward-lifting low level jet. Over the course of the next several hours, numerous strong to occasionally severe storms continued spreading north and east across the area, with half dollar size hail reported as far north as southern Greeley County. Within the aforementioned primary zone of heavy rain, the majority fell between 2:30-6:00 a.m. CDT. As one area of storms was in the process of exiting eastern counties of the local area around sunrise, a separate, quick-moving line of non-severe convection moved in from the southwest, sweeping across most of South Central Nebraska between 5-9 a.m. CDT. Although a few storms persisted here and there through late-morning, most places began a brief respite before the next round of evening/overnight storms arrived. Turning to the synoptic and mesoscale situation: with a generally west-east oriented surface front residing near the Nebraska-Kansas border through the night, seasonably-strong instability and shear in place, and a strengthening 30-40 knot southerly low level jet taking direct aim on the local area, this was a well-anticipated nocturnal convective flare-up. On the large scale, the Central Plains was under broad west-southwest flow aloft, well-downstream from a large trough centered over the West Coast. At the time of the first severe-criteria hail reports around 11:30 p.m. CDT, the environment featured most-unstable CAPE of 1000-2000 J/kg and 0-6 kilometer deep-layer wind shear of 30-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar size hail occurred...report relayed by broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the third consecutive night, this time from the evening of Thursday the 4th into the early morning of Friday the 5th, several rounds of thunderstorms rumbled across much of the 24-county South Central Nebraska area. And for the second straight night, the majority of storms concentrated within the southeastern half of the area. Although the first few hours of evening convection yielded several hail reports up to mainly quarter size within Hall, Hamilton, Merrick and York counties, by far the biggest story of the night, and for some places for the next several days thereafter, was heavy rainfall and resultant flooding. During the 24-hour period ending around sunrise on the 5th, widespread rainfall of 2-4 drenched several counties, primarily including: Adams, Clay, Fillmore, York, Nuckolls, Thayer, southern Hall and southern Hamilton. Combined with as much as 1.50-3.00 that fell across some of these same areas during the preceding night, the net result was 36-hour rain totals of generally 3-7, with the overall-highest concentration of 5-7 impacting a fairly narrow, roughly 10-mile wide corridor extending through southeast Hall, northeast Adams, southern Hamilton, northern Clay and western Fillmore counties. As just a few specific examples of 36-hour measured totals ending around sunrise on the 5th: 6.44 at Harvard and 5.28 four miles west of Geneva (NeRAIN observers). For the calendar days of June 3rd-5th, Hastings airport totaled 5.39, of which 4.74 fell on the 4th. This total set a new Hastings daily record for June rainfall, and was the third-highest daily total for any day of the year. Needless to say, widespread flooding of creeks, streams and primarily rural roadways was well-underway in the aforementioned counties by daybreak on the 5th. Adams county Emergency Management issued a press release alerting citizens that some roads are impassable and dangerous due to standing water and possible unknown damage in the northeast part of the county. Earlier on, during the initial stages of heavy rain on the evening of the 4th, significant street flooding impacted many communities including Grand Island, where several city streets were closed for a few hours. Also near Grand Island on the evening of the 4th, the Platte River (which had already been slightly above flood stage for a few weeks) briefly rose far enough out of its banks to overflow Locust Street near the Interstate 80 exit 314 interchange. Getting back to the daytime hours on the 5th, although flooding of roads and small streams began to very slowly subside, issues began ramping up on larger creeks and rivers. Most notably, this included the West Fork Big Blue River, which was centered almost directly under the narrow axis of heaviest rain and experienced historic flooding over the next several days (please refer to the separate paragraph below for more details regarding West Fork Big Blue River flooding).||Taking a closer look at the timing and evolution of local thunderstorm activity from the afternoon of the 4th into the early morning of the 5th...there were several different rounds. Following a brief lull in the wake of widespread activity during the early morning of the 4th, the next round of mainly non-severe storms affected counties primarily along/north of Interstate 80 through most of the afternoon hours. These storms yielded only one severe weather report: quarter size hail near Pleasanton. Following another brief lull during the late afternoon and early evening, a narrow swath of hail and torrential rain-producing severe storms blew up between 7-9 p.m. CDT along an axis from near Grand Island and Central City, eastward to near Stromsburg and York. Between 9-11 p.m. CDT, this vigorous line of convection gradually weakened as it sank southward toward the Kansas border, but continued dumping heavy rain. Beyond 11 p.m., the next several hours featured widespread, persistent development of non-severe-but-heavy-rain producing convection over most counties along and south of Interstate 80, largely driven by convergence/lift along the nose of a nocturnal, 30-40 knot low level jet. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of heavy rainfall totaled generally 4-7 across primarily southern Hamilton County between the 3rd-6th, with the majority of this rain falling on the 4th-5th. Localized road flooding was initially reported across parts of the county on the evening of the 4th. Over the next few days, the main story turned to the West Fork Big Blue River and its smaller stream tributaries in the southern part of the county, as the cumulative effects of multiple rounds of heavy rain resulted in significant flooding, mainly on the 5th-6th. Reports indicated that this may have been some of the worst flooding observed along the river in 50-80 years. A few days of warm and dry weather settled in from June 7-9, allowing flood waters to largely recede.

In what had already been a very active start to June across parts of South Central Nebraska, what transpired on the afternoon of Wednesday the 10th into the early morning of Thursday the 11th was the final, truly widespread severe weather/flooding event of the month before a noticeably quieter weather pattern settled in over the next few weeks. Focusing first on the severe weather aspect, the afternoon and evening featured several reports of large hail and damaging winds within various counties. Storm mode was primarily multicell, some of which started out as semi-discrete before eventually merging into larger clusters and lines. Most hail reports were in the nickel to half dollar size range, with 1.25 diameter stones reported near Elwood and Lexington. However, one storm that tracked north of Lexington around 6 p.m. CDT was a prolific hail-producer, as a storm chaser reported a short stretch of Highway 21 completely hail-covered with most stones averaging golf ball size but one as large as 3 in diameter. As for damaging winds, there were primarily three noteworthy storms. First off, a cluster of severe storms shifted eastward into the Valley/Sherman counties area between 5:30-6:30 p.m. CDT. In Arcadia, the NWS cooperative observer estimated 70 MPH gusts, with 10-12 diameter tree limbs down. A bit later, a storm became severe near the Franklin/Kearney County line around 7 p.m. CDT and rapidly tracked northeast during the next few hours. Along the way, estimated 70 MPH winds overturned seven empty freight cars of a 72-car train near Kenesaw in extreme western Adams County. This storm weakened before reaching Grand Island, with only 50-60 MPH gusts reported on the south side of town around 8:30 p.m. CDT. While the train-toppling storm was in progress, a separate severe storm to its northeast passed through Merrick and northern Hamilton counties, causing tree and power line damage in Central City and Hordville around 8 p.m. CDT. By 9 p.m. CDT, storms had largely settled below severe-limits, with just one additional 60 MPH wind report along the Franklin/Webster County line. Through the remainder of the night, scattered to numerous showers and non-severe storms persisted over much of South Central Nebraska, with most counties finally rain-free by sunrise. As for rainfall amounts, the majority of the 24-county area totaled 1-2. However, there were a few smaller pockets of at least 2-3, most notably within York, northern Hamilton and southwestern Polk counties, where NeRAIN observers reported 3.46 near Hordville and 3.24 near Benedict.  Although a few dry days leading up to this event had allowed the York County area to start drying out in the wake of flooding rains that fell primarily on the 4th-5th, this event resulted in renewed short-term flooding issues. Most notably this involved the West Fork Big Blue River near McCool Junction, which again came out of its banks during the day on the 11th, only a few days after historic flooding occurred. Fortunately, flooding was not as severe this time around, and the river largely fell back into its banks by the end of the day on Friday the 12th (please refer to the June 4th p.m.-June 5th a.m. episode narrative for much more detail regarding multiple days of flooding along the West Fork Big Blue River). ||Most of the timing details of this severe weather event were already covered in the preceding paragraph, as all hail/strong wind reports occurred between 4:00-9:30 p.m. CDT. However, the first strong storms of the afternoon actually developed eastward into the Dawson/Gosper counties area as early as 2:30-4:00 p.m. CDT. Turning to the synoptic and mesoscale setup: the primary trigger aloft was a series of fairly subtle disturbances gradually overspreading the Central Plains, out ahead of a modest shortwave trough drifting southeast toward the region from the Northern Rockies. At the surface, a few different boundaries helped to focus convection, including a generally west-to-east oriented quasi-stationary front draped through central Nebraska and also a trough axis extending southwest from the local area to weak low pressure centered near the KS/CO border. With afternoon temperatures rising between 90-95 degrees, storms erupted in an environment featuring around 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 30-40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear. During the night, widespread weak convection persisted within the exit region of a 30-40 knot low level jet.
