EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving through the central plains allowed for low pressure to develop over the high Plains, and a warm front to lift north into the northern Plains. Thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon along a sharping dry line over central Plains and moved northeast. Moisture and thunderstorms moved into eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa during the late afternoon hours. As shear increased during the evening isolated severe weather was observed over southeast Nebraska, mainly in the form of strong winds and 1 EF1 tornado. Although isolated severe weather occurred, training thunderstorms resulting in extremely heavy rainfall and flash flooding across many areas of southeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported that flash flooding continued as heavy rainfall continued during the overnight. The flooding destroyed a bridge along state highway 15 in northern Jefferson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with the storm system that brought the heavy rainfall to the area on the evening of the 6th, moved through southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa on the afternoon of the 7th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along the cold front by mid afternoon and continued into the early early evening hours. Isolated storms produced marginal severe weather in both southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.

A cold front associated with the storm system that brought the heavy rainfall to the area on the evening of the 6th, moved through southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa on the afternoon of the 7th. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along the cold front by mid afternoon and continued into the early early evening hours. Isolated storms produced marginal severe weather in both southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported tree limbs were blown down. The spotter estimated winds near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With ground already saturated from repeated heavy rainfall events in May and early June, another complex of thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall moved across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa starting during the evening on the 10th and continued into the early morning hours on the 11th. This was followed with additional thunderstorms on the afternoon of the 11th over southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. This additional rainfall led to more significant flooding along area rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the second, and more significant flood of the Missouri River near Rulo in the month of June. The river crested at 21.47 ft, or 5.47 feet above flood stage. In addition to significant agricultural low-land flooding, some cabins along the river also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms formed on the evening of March 28th across the eastern Nebraska Panhandle. As they moved east, they produced a downburst over portions of Garden County which led to damage.  As the thunderstorms moved east, they touched off a wildfire in far eastern Arthur County.  Aided by strong northerly winds, the fire burned over 35,000 acres of range land in eastern Arthur, western McPherson and eastern Keith Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dry thunderstorm produced down burstwinds estimated at 60 MPH.  Tree limbs of 4 to 5 inches in diameter were downed.  Pieces of tin blew off of a calving shed and a wooden fence was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across Lincoln County to produce large hail.

An intense late season snowstorm affected portions of the western Nebraska Panhandle with moderate to heavy snow and gusty northeast winds of 30 mph. Very low visibility in falling and blowing snow closed portions of Highway 385 north of Alliance. Snowfall totals ranged from six inches to as much as two feet north of the Pine Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow was reported six miles south of Harrisburg.

A powerful upper level low crossed western Nebraska late on May 9th to produce heavy snow overnight in Sheridan County. The precipitation began as rain, mixed with snow and transitioned to all snow. The heaviest snow up to 6 inches fells in the Pine Ridge area where localized amounts up to a foot were expected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell with 6 inch reports averaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With ground already saturated from repeated heavy rainfall events in May and early June, another complex of thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall moved across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa starting during the evening on the 10th and continued into the early morning hours on the 11th. This was followed with additional thunderstorms on the afternoon of the 11th over southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. This additional rainfall led to more significant flooding along area rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the second significant flood of June along Turkey Creek, and the third of the year. The river crested over 4 feet above flood stage, and although briefly feel below flood stage on the 13th, additional heavy rain on the 14th allowed the creek to stay above flood stage until the 18th. Significant flooding of row crops in the flood plain was observed from Wilbur downstream to De Witt where some structures near town were again flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon of the 25th as modified gulf moisture returned north into the middle Missouri River Valley. As moisture returned to the area, instability increased and allowed for the thunderstorm development. The majority of the thunderstorms were non-severe in nature due to the weak atmospheric shear and limited instability, but isolated large hail was observed in southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 4-9 p.m. CDT on this Sunday afternoon and evening, a smattering of strong to severe thunderstorms developed along and just ahead of an invading cold front, producing hail in various parts of South Central Nebraska, but especially within Polk, Nance and northeast Merrick counties in far northeast portions of the local area. Within these counties, the main event occurred between 430-630 p.m. CDT as a cluster of semi-discrete severe storms blossomed slightly ahead of the cold front and tracked east-northeast before exiting into eastern Nebraska. Polk County yielded the most hail reports, with nickel to quarter size stones reported in the Osceola and Shelby areas, and larger golf ball size stones reported in western and far northeast sections of the county. In fact, a NeRAIN observer located a few miles south-southwest of Columbus reported broken windows and roof damage in the area. Outside of this main, concentrated zone of severe storms, a combination of linear and small multicell storm structures yielded a few instances of sub-severe hail in the Ord, Red Cloud and Hubbell areas, and a lone quarter size report in Clay Center. By 9 p.m. CDT, the surface cold front had pushed through all of South Central Nebraska, ending all local thunderstorm activity for the night as convection exited Fillmore and Thayer counties to the east. Rainfall-wise, the vast majority of affected areas received well under 1. ||In the mid-upper levels, forcing was provided by the southern reaches of a broad shortwave trough tracking east across the Central and especially Northern Plains, with this trough ushering in increasing west-southwest flow aloft. At the surface, the well-defined, northeast-southwest oriented cold front steadily swept through South Central Nebraska during the afternoon and evening. Ahead of the boundary, afternoon temperatures across most of the local area topped out between 75-80 F as dewpoints rose to between 50-55 F on gusty south winds. By the time storms initiated, the combination of mixed-layer CAPE values on the order of 1000 J/kg and 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-50 knots was plenty sufficient for large hail production.

During the late afternoon and evening hours on this Thursday, scattered to numerous thunderstorms rumbled through South Central Nebraska counties mainly along and south of the Interstate 80 corridor, resulting in a handful of sub-severe hail reports in the penny to nickel size range. Although radar data suggested that various other locations likely experienced hail up to around this size, the only ground-truth reports originated from Hastings, Edgar and from the vicinities of Kearney, Sutton and Clay Center. While most of this convection was multicellular and linear, there were a few briefly semi-discrete structures as well. Rainfall amounts under most of the heaviest convection averaged between 0.50 and 1, with only few localized higher exceptions. ||Strong storms first developed into southwestern counties such as Harlan and Franklin between 430-600 p.m. CDT. Then between 6-9 p.m. CDT, a couple of narrow west-to-east arcs of strong storms developed eastward through the Highway 6 and Interstate 80 corridors and lifted slowly north, resulting in the aforementioned hail reports. After nightfall, a few stronger storms lingered over mainly Thayer and Fillmore counties for a time, but these steadily faded with the loss of diurnal heating. In the mid and upper levels, weak forcing was provided by small-scale disturbances shifting into the Central Plains on southerly flow aloft, well-downstream from an expansive large-scale closed low spinning over the Four Corners region. At the surface, afternoon high temperatures only climbed between the mid-60s and low-70s F across most of the area, but the combination of surface dewpoints in the mid-50s and rather cold air aloft (evidenced by 500 millibar temps around -16 C) was sufficient to promote modest surface-based CAPE as high as 500-1000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear only around 30 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell on the west side of Hastings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On this Wednesday morning, a fairly significant winter storm took aim on South Central Nebraska for the second time in five days. Unlike the more prolonged event of the preceding weekend, this one was a quick hitter associated with one primary snow band, with most affected areas receiving accumulation for no more than 6-7 hours. Despite this brevity, bursts of moderate to heavy snow combined with sustained north wind of generally 20-25 MPH and gusts up to around 35 MPH to promote a treacherous morning commute along with numerous school closures. The brunt of this system targeted locations north-northeast of a line extending from Gothenburg-Hastings-Hebron, where most places received at least 2-4 inches. However, the epicenter of heaviest accumulations on the order of 4-8 occurred in a generally 30-40 mile wide corridor extending from around Loup City in the northwest, then east-southeast through the St. Paul, Grand Island, Central City, Aurora, Osceola and York areas. Within the heart of this band, a few of the highest measured totals (per a combination of NWS cooperative observers and NeRAIN observers) featured 8.7 four miles north of Aurora, 8.2 at Ravenna, 7.1 in Loup City, 6.1 at Grand Island airport, 6.0 at St. Paul and 5.7 in Osceola. On either side of this primary snow band there was a fairly sharp drop-off in amounts, as evidenced by reports from Ord (3.0) and Genoa (3.0) to the north, and Hastings (1.7) and Hebron (2.8) to the south. ||Breaking down event timing, this generally west-to-east oriented snow band first infiltrated the northwestern quadrant of South Central Nebraska between 1-3 a.m. and then steadily intensified through daybreak as it spread east. Generally between 7-9 a.m., the band began sinking southward across the Highway 6 corridor and toward the Kansas border, while gradually weakening and ending from north-to-south and also from west-to-east. By 1130 a.m. the vast majority of the local area was snow-free. On the synoptic and mesoscale aloft, this quick-but-intense event was driven by a rather classic, strongly-forced frontogenetic band evident mainly in the 850-500 millibar layer, but especially centered around 700 millibars. This frontogenetic forcing slid across the heart of Nebraska from northwest-to-southeast, enhanced by a coupled upper jet structure evident at 300 millibars, featuring the exit region of one jet streak nosing in from the Northern Rockies and the entrance region of another jet streak centered over the Upper Midwest. At the surface, gusty north wind across Nebraska was driven by an enhanced pressure gradient between an Arctic high pressure system sliding into the Northern Plains and the leading edges of a cold front charging southward through southern Kansas at daybreak. Temperatures across South Central Nebraska averaged between 15-20 F during the majority of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 3.0 inches was measured in Genoa. Wind gusts of up to 30 to 35 mph occurred out of the north.

Generally between 7-9 a.m., the band began sinking southward across the Highway 6 corridor and toward the Kansas border, while gradually weakening and ending from north-to-south and also from west-to-east. By 1130 a.m. the vast majority of the local area was snow-free. On the synoptic and mesoscale aloft, this quick-but-intense event was driven by a rather classic, strongly-forced frontogenetic band evident mainly in the 850-500 millibar layer, but especially centered around 700 millibars. This frontogenetic forcing slid across the heart of Nebraska from northwest-to-southeast, enhanced by a coupled upper jet structure evident at 300 millibars, featuring the exit region of one jet streak nosing in from the Northern Rockies and the entrance region of another jet streak centered over the Upper Midwest. At the surface, gusty north wind across Nebraska was driven by an enhanced pressure gradient between an Arctic high pressure system sliding into the Northern Plains and the leading edges of a cold front charging southward through southern Kansas at daybreak. Temperatures across South Central Nebraska averaged between 15-20 F during the majority of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 4.0 inches was measured in Geneva. Wind gusts of up to 30 to 35 mph occurred out of the north.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe storms developed to produce large hail in Boyd County.
An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail in the community of Hershey.

Scattered to numerous, semi-discrete and multicell thunderstorms blossomed across much of South Central Nebraska on this Saturday afternoon and evening, with a few storms becoming severe and producing hail ranging from 1-2 in diameter. The first severe storm of the afternoon developed near Kearney around 5 p.m. CDT, and was likely the most prolific hail-producer as it slowly drifted north-northwest through central and northwest Buffalo County for nearly two hours. Just north of Kearney, a few vehicles slid off of Highway 10 as it became hail-covered and slick. This storm also produced the largest reported hail of the day (2) north of Riverdale. Between 6-9 p.m. CDT, several other storms either flared up over various parts of the area or pushed northward out of Kansas, with quarter size hail reported near the state line south of Red Cloud and also in Wood River. Even though the mesoscale environment was potentially supportive of a somewhat greater severe weather threat, the fairly rapid development of plentiful convection and resultant storm-scale outflow interference likely mitigated this. With the loss of diurnal heating, storms steadily weakened and diminished in coverage within a few hours after nightfall. Although most affected areas received less than 1 of rainfall, much of northern Kearney County along with adjacent portions of southeast Buffalo, southwest Hall and northwest Adams counties received higher amounts to around 2. ||In the mid-upper levels, the primary player was an expansive closed low churning slowly eastward through eastern Colorado, with several smaller-scale disturbances shifting northward into the Central Plains along its eastern flank. At the surface, there were no distinct synoptic features within South Central Nebraska, only fairly weak, broad low pressure and prevailing light southerly to easterly breezes. Despite afternoon temperatures only rising into the low-70s F, the combination of surface dewpoints in the low to mid-50s and notably cold air aloft (evidenced by 500 millibar temps around -18 C) yielded surface-based CAPE around 1000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to golf ball size covered Highway 10 three miles north of Kearney, causing drivers to slide off of the road and into the ditch.

During the late afternoon and evening hours on this Thursday, scattered to numerous thunderstorms rumbled through South Central Nebraska counties mainly along and south of the Interstate 80 corridor, resulting in a handful of sub-severe hail reports in the penny to nickel size range. Although radar data suggested that various other locations likely experienced hail up to around this size, the only ground-truth reports originated from Hastings, Edgar and from the vicinities of Kearney, Sutton and Clay Center. While most of this convection was multicellular and linear, there were a few briefly semi-discrete structures as well. Rainfall amounts under most of the heaviest convection averaged between 0.50 and 1, with only few localized higher exceptions. ||Strong storms first developed into southwestern counties such as Harlan and Franklin between 430-600 p.m. CDT. Then between 6-9 p.m. CDT, a couple of narrow west-to-east arcs of strong storms developed eastward through the Highway 6 and Interstate 80 corridors and lifted slowly north, resulting in the aforementioned hail reports. After nightfall, a few stronger storms lingered over mainly Thayer and Fillmore counties for a time, but these steadily faded with the loss of diurnal heating. In the mid and upper levels, weak forcing was provided by small-scale disturbances shifting into the Central Plains on southerly flow aloft, well-downstream from an expansive large-scale closed low spinning over the Four Corners region. At the surface, afternoon high temperatures only climbed between the mid-60s and low-70s F across most of the area, but the combination of surface dewpoints in the mid-50s and rather cold air aloft (evidenced by 500 millibar temps around -16 C) was sufficient to promote modest surface-based CAPE as high as 500-1000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear only around 30 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail nearly covered the ground. Most of the hail stones were pea size, but some were as big as three quarter inch diameter. Wind gusts of up to 30 mph and torrential rain occurred, and visibility dropped to about 3/8 of a mile during the heaviest rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed about four miles east-southeast of the Sidney Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An evening thunderstorm produced large hail over northern Box Butte County in the northern Nebraska Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported about six miles south-southwest of Box Butte Reservoir Campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon and early evening thunderstorms produced strong winds and large hail across portions of the western Nebraska Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail covered the ground 13 miles north of Potter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon and early evening thunderstorms produced strong winds and large hail across portions of the western Nebraska Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed eight miles south of Potter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced predominantly damaging winds on this Friday afternoon and evening. An area of showers moved into central Nebraska at mid-afternoon. A lone thunderstorm developed on the southern edge of these showers and briefly became severe over Merrick County, producing hail up to the size of quarters. The main event occurred later in the evening, beginning with several multicell storms that developed from Dawson County west into southern Lincoln County. These storms were initially not severe, but as they headed east, new storms initiated downstream, forming a large cluster of multicell storms. These storms produced multiple reports of strong winds and wind damage over Hall, Hamilton, Clay, York, and Fillmore Counties, mostly along and within 15 miles on either side of Interstate 80. These storms were severe in the 8 to 10 p.m. CST time frame. Tree limbs of various sizes were reported down in multiple locations and winds were measured as high as 62 mph in Grand Island and 59 mph in York and Henderson. There were a couple reports of hail. The largest was the size of ping pong balls in Heartwell, which is in Kearney county.||These storms formed just north of a weak cold front that dropped through most of Nebraska during the morning hours. In the upper levels, a broad ridge encompassed the Plains atop a subtropical high over the Southern Plains. The Central Plains were on the southern fringe of the Westerlies. Surface temperatures in advance of the storms were in the mid 80s, with dewpoints near 70. With mid-level lapse rates of 7.5 deg C/km, MLCAPE values were between 2000 and 3000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was between 30 and 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 65 MPH resulted in some shingles being blown off the roof of a local business and tree branches being downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Primarily between 4-8 p.m. CDT on this Sunday, several multicell thunderstorms rumbled across various portions of South Central Nebraska, but with the overall-greatest coverage focused within counties north of Interstate 80. While most of these storms produced nothing more than small hail and non-severe wind gusts, one flared up to severe levels over Sherman County mainly between 5:30-6:00 p.m. CDT, yielding a couple quarter size hail reports from the Loup City and Ashton areas. A few miles south of Ashton, one resident reported vinyl siding damage along with a destroyed garden. As the early evening hours wore on, this scattered convection steadily faded away with the loss of daytime heating. ||In the most basic sense, this largely diurnally-driven convection was sparked by a combination of strong surface heating and high instability near a weak, west-east oriented cold front sagging down from northern Nebraska, along with limited support aloft from a subtle disturbance embedded within broad northwesterly flow. Despite somewhat warm mid level temperatures, the combination of afternoon highs rising well into the 90s F and dewpoints averaging in the mid-60s to low-70s helped promote considerable instability with mixed-layer CAPE on the order of 2000-4000 J/kg. Storm organization was likely limited somewhat by only modest deep-layer wind shear generally on the order of 30-35 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The vinyl siding on the west side of the garage was damaged by the hail. The garden located at the home was destroyed and there was notable crop damage in the area.

A couple severe thunderstorms produced hail up to one inch in diameter on this Monday afternoon and evening, as well as a little wind damage. Scattered mostly single cell thunderstorms developed during the early to mid-afternoon hours, with a few small multicell clusters. These storms formed over the Sandhills and progressed southeastward through the late afternoon and evening hours, affecting portions of central and south central Nebraska. One of the storms that became severe moved out of western Polk County around 3:45 p.m. CST, and then dropped almost due south through York County. This storm blew an irrigation pivot over near Benedict and produced hail up to one inch in diameter just north of York.  One storm produced wind strong enough to snap off some 5 inch diameter tree limbs in Central City. As this storm continued southeastward, it produced hail up to the size of quarters in Henderson.||These storms developed along and head of a weak cold front which was sagging into Nebraska from the north. Northwest flow was established over the Plains, with a longwave ridge over the western United States, and a trough over the East. A subtropical high was situated over the Desert Southwest. Despite poor mid-level lapse rates, temperatures in the lower 90s, with dewpoints in the upper 60s, resulted in MLCAPE values of 2000-3000 J/kg. Deep layer shear increased from 25 kts, early in the event, to around 30 knots during its peak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area pivot was overturned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This Sunday afternoon and evening featured a rather localized-but-significant flooding event within primarily central and northeast portions of Furnas County, including the community of Edison, which was ground zero for flooding rains and also a brief bout of severe wind gusts estimated up to 65 MPH, along with hail up to quarter size. According to a trained spotter two miles southwest of Edison, an unofficial rain total of 5 inches poured down in a 90-minute span between 3:30-5:00 p.m. CDT, washing out part of a driveway. In Edison itself, the NWS cooperative observer recorded a slightly lower storm total of 3.74, but this was still more than enough rain in a short time to cause significant flooding of several roads and some structures. Some of the worst damage occurred at Ag Valley Coop, where a shallow depth of water infiltrated much of the first floor office. Even after the last drops of lingering light rain vacated Edison around 9 p.m. CDT, impactful flooding persisted well into the morning hours of the 17th. Although the Furnas County flooding situation was the big story from this event, radar data suggested that other pockets of flooding might have also occurred in portions of eastern Gosper and western Phelps counties, where rain totals of at least 2-3 were also fairly common. ||For the vast majority of the 16th, all the way from the pre-dawn hours through midnight, at least isolated to scattered showers and mainly weak thunderstorms affected at least some portion of South Central Nebraska, primarily within counties northwest of line from Franklin to York. While most of this convection was fairly disorganized and weak, the mid-afternoon through early evening hours featured a noticeable uptick in storm coverage and intensity, especially in southwestern counties such as Furnas, Gosper, and Phelps. Within this area, an initially weak cluster of convection rapidly intensified between 3-6 p.m. CDT, morphing into a nearly solid, southward-sinking line of slow-moving strong to severe storms. The primary synoptic scale trigger for this event was a very slow-moving, southwest-to-northeast oriented surface cold front roughly bisecting Nebraska during the afternoon and evening. Considerable moisture pooled in the vicinity of this boundary, featuring surface dewpoints in the mid-60s to low-70s F and precipitable water values averaging 1.50-2.00. In the mid-upper levels, forcing was rather subtle and weak, with southern Nebraska positioned well-south of a primary shortwave trough shifting from the Northern Rockies into the Dakotas. Around the time of the most intense Furnas County convection, mesoscale parameters featured at least 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE, but weak deep-layer wind shear only around 25 knots, helping promote slow storm motions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph were reported both from law enforcement two miles north of Edison, and also a trained spotter in Edison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front sagged southward through western Iowa and eastern Nebraska during the day on August 2nd. Ahead of the front during the early morning hours the nocturnal low-level jet increased ahead of the front. This allowed for isolated thunderstorms to develop in northeast Nebraska and move into west central Iowa during the early morning hours. Some of these thunderstorms reached severe limits with large hail the main threats. These thunderstorms dissipated in the morning hours, but as the instability increased during the afternoon thunderstorms redeveloped along the cold front. Vertical wind shear also increased during the afternoon as well with some of the thunderstorms becoming supercells producing large hail and damaging winds. The thunderstorms weakened by mid to late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three sections of the center pivot were overturned in a field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A seasonably strong jet stream was moving across the northern Plains during the day on the 8th of August. During the late afternoon and evening a warm front lifted north into southeast Nebraska as low pressure associated with the jet streak moved into eastern Nebraska. Thunderstorms developed by mid evening along the warm front producing an isolated tornado and locally heavy rainfall across southeast Nebraska and into southwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed in the extreme northeast part of Pawnee County from a supercell moving southeast. The tornado only occurred for a short distance in Pawnee County before moving into Richardson County. Little damage occurred in in Pawnee County but the tornado was given an EF1 rating based on how extensive the crop damage was from the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms roamed through primarily the western half of South Central Nebraska (west of the Highway 281 corridor) during the morning and early afternoon hours on this Thursday. Although the majority of storms produced no more than small hail and brief downpours, one organized, elevated supercell became marginally severe as it tracked east-southeast across portions of Furnas and Harlan counties between 9:00-11:30 a.m. CDT before departing southward into Kansas. Actually, this storm first developed just west of the local area over the Frontier/Red Willow County line. Ground-truth reports were limited, but nickel size hail was reported southeast of Cambridge and quarter size hail near the state line south of Alma. In Beaver City, only pea size hail was reported, but the NWS cooperative observer tallied 1.77 inches of rain in 40 minutes. In the wake of this supercell, a few additional strong storms flared up within the area into early afternoon, but all of South Central Nebraska was pretty much storm-free by 2 p.m. CDT. ||The development of this isolated severe storm was fairly unexpected, given rather weak forcing aloft and also warm mid level temperatures. However, in the mid-upper levels, the broad quasi-zonal flow in place over Nebraska did in fact feature a subtle disturbance moving in from the west, along the southern periphery of a better-defined, low amplitude shortwave trough tracking out of the Northern Rockies into the Dakotas. Despite temperatures at the 700-millibar level averaging at least 12-14 C, this rogue severe storm thrived in a mesoscale environment featuring most-unstable CAPE around 2000 J/kg and seasonably strong deep-layer wind shear of 40-50 knots.

Severe thunderstorms produced hail up to the size of golf balls in a few locations on this Tuesday afternoon. Just after 2 p.m. CST, an isolated thunderstorm developed along the Buffalo-Sherman County line. This storm advanced slowly to the east and weakened by 3:30 p.m. By 2:45 p.m., another storm joined it over northern Sherman County. This storm moved north into Valley County by 3:30 p.m. and exited the county by 5 p.m. Both of these storms produced hail up to the size of golf balls. The second storm produced golf ball size hail near Arcadia and near Fort Hartsuff State Park. ||These storms formed well ahead of a backdoor cold front that was pressing into northeastern Nebraska. However, convective initiation was more likely a result of a small area of low-level convergence between Broken Bow, where winds were from the west, and Ord, where winds were from the east. In the upper levels, a subtropical high was over Oklahoma, with a ridge extending north through the Dakotas. Troughs were located over the western and eastern United States. At mid-afternoon, surface temperatures were in the middle 90s, with dewpoints in the middle to upper 60s. With mid-level lapse rates near 7 deg C/km, MLCAPE was around 2500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was near 25 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranged from quarter to golf ball size.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The storm survey found tree damage consistent with straight line winds in this area.

During the late afternoon and evening a warm front lifted north into southeast Nebraska as low pressure associated with the jet streak moved into eastern Nebraska. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado continued into Richardson County from Pawnee County. The tornado continue to produce little damage in Richardson County as there were little structures for the tornado to impact. Some tree damage was noted along the path in Richardson County as well as continued crop damage. As the tornado dissipated it made a loop near 632nd Ave and move southwest for a short time before finally dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moving quickly through the northern Plains allowed for a seasonably strong cold front to sweep through eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa late in the day on the 9th of August. Isolated to scattered supercells developed along the cold front as it moved southeast across the area with some of the supercells producing severe weather in the form of large hail and damaging winds, as well as locally heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level storm system moved across the northern Plains during the day on August 22nd. This system allowed for a strong cold front to sweep across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the late afternoon and evening. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed along the cold front from west central Iowa into eastern Nebraska, and moved southeast through the evening. Although the large majority of these storms were sub-severe, isolated reports of large hail and damaging winds were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reports in town indicated hail ranged from quarter to golf ball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level storm system crossed Nebraska during the day on August 28th and into the state of Iowa on the 29th. Seasonably deep moisture combined with the slow moving storm system to produce intense rainfall over parts of northeast Nebraska. Some rainfall amounts ranged from 6 to 8 inches during the evening. The rainfall slowly diminished after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement, as well as local print media, indicated that flash flooding occurred from heavy repeated thunderstorms across parts of Antelope County. The worst of the flooding was near the areas of Oakdale and Elgin. The flooding led to water covering U.S. Highway 275 and State Highway 70 into the morning on the 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening a line of severe storms moved into Southwest Nebraska from Northwest Kansas.  The highest wind speeds were along the leading edge of the squall line.  The highest wind gust reported was 100 MPH at Stratton.  Large hail was also reported as the squall line moved through, with the golf ball size hail near Stratton being the largest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high wind gust snapped the top of an 80 foot tree off, with the top of the tree flying over a nearby house.  The wind also flipped a swing set.  The house shook because the winds were so strong.  Elsewhere around town there was widespread, major tree damage.  Twelve to eighteen inch diameter trees were snapped and uprooted.  Power poles were damaged which lead to power outages in town and the surrounding area.  In town one garage and one unanchored mobile home were destroyed.  There were many roofs damaged as well as siding on houses.  A camper was overturned, damaged and resulted in one (direct) fatality.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 60 MPH resulted in tree damage and power outages in Aurora.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was a rather active weather night for South Central Nebraska, highlighted by a handful of severe-criteria wind reports within counties along and south of Interstate 80. Although widespread precipitation affected the area for several hours on this Friday night into early Saturday morning, all severe wind reports occurred between 11:30 p.m. and 12:30 a.m. CDT as a north-to-south oriented bowing segment charged through. Along and near the leading edge of the surging outflow, wind gusts around 60 MPH were reported near Gibbon, in rural southeastern Adams and southwestern Clay counties, and also near Superior. Well-behind this bowing line, severe gusts associated with a well-defined mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) brought down numerous tree limbs in Cozad. Although there were no confirmed tornadoes, the presence of seasonably-strong low level shear aided the development of a few brief, radar-indicated rotational signatures, one of which prompted a short-fused Tornado Warning for southern Adams County. Rainfall-wise, though many areas roughly southwest of a Ravenna-Hastings-Hebron line picked up 1-2, the fairly progressive nature of the heaviest cores held flooding issues to a minimum, save for typical short-term street flooding in communities such as Elm Creek. ||Timing-wise, most of South Central Nebraska remained rain-free until after dark, save for a narrow band of weak convection that lifted through several northern and eastern counties during the early evening. However, a pair of expanding severe storm clusters gradually took aim on the local area as they approached from southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas, eventually merging into an organized mesoscale convective system (MCS) that surged into western portions of South Central Nebraska between 10-11 p.m. CDT. By the midnight to 1 a.m. CDT time frame, the primary bowing segment raced through counties south of Interstate 80 and east of Highway 281 before departing into southeast Nebraska and northeast Kansas. In its wake, a large shield of light to moderate rain persisted across the local area for most of the night. In the mid/upper levels, this event featured slightly-stronger-than-average forcing by early August standards, as a well-defined lead disturbance moved out of the Central Rockies into the Central Plains in quasi-zonal flow aloft, downstream from a larger scale parent trough centered over Nevada. By the time the MCS entered the local area, convection was being sustained/enhanced by a 30-40 knot southerly low level jet evident at 850 millibars. At the surface, South Central Nebraska featured a light easterly flow regime, to the north of a quasi-stationary front loosely draped across the southern half of Kansas. Around the time of the severe wind reports, mesoscale parameters featured 1000-2000 J/kg most unstable CAPE, deep layer wind shear of 30-40 knots and rather strong 0-1 kilometer low level shear of 20-30 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH at campground area near Interstate 80 exit 285.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms roamed through primarily the western half of South Central Nebraska (west of the Highway 281 corridor) during the morning and early afternoon hours on this Thursday. Although the majority of storms produced no more than small hail and brief downpours, one organized, elevated supercell became marginally severe as it tracked east-southeast across portions of Furnas and Harlan counties between 9:00-11:30 a.m. CDT before departing southward into Kansas. Actually, this storm first developed just west of the local area over the Frontier/Red Willow County line. Ground-truth reports were limited, but nickel size hail was reported southeast of Cambridge and quarter size hail near the state line south of Alma. In Beaver City, only pea size hail was reported, but the NWS cooperative observer tallied 1.77 inches of rain in 40 minutes. In the wake of this supercell, a few additional strong storms flared up within the area into early afternoon, but all of South Central Nebraska was pretty much storm-free by 2 p.m. CDT. ||The development of this isolated severe storm was fairly unexpected, given rather weak forcing aloft and also warm mid level temperatures. However, in the mid-upper levels, the broad quasi-zonal flow in place over Nebraska did in fact feature a subtle disturbance moving in from the west, along the southern periphery of a better-defined, low amplitude shortwave trough tracking out of the Northern Rockies into the Dakotas. Despite temperatures at the 700-millibar level averaging at least 12-14 C, this rogue severe storm thrived in a mesoscale environment featuring most-unstable CAPE around 2000 J/kg and seasonably strong deep-layer wind shear of 40-50 knots.

Quarter size hail was reported in two locations on this Saturday evening. Late in the afternoon, scattered thunderstorms developed between Ord and McCook. The storms were brief and quickly dissipated along and south of Interstate 80. To the north of the Interstate, instability was greater. The storms continued and strengthened, with a couple briefly turning severe, producing hail up to 1 inch in diameter near Greeley and Clarks. ||These storms formed within a broad warm sector, ahead of the lee-side trough that became mobile and advanced east into the central third of Nebraska. There was no discernible forcing aloft as the flow was zonal, and the subtropical and polar jet streams were far to the south and north, respectively. Surface dewpoint depressions were large, with temperatures in the mid 80s and dewpoints in the upper 40s. However, mid-level lapse rates were around 7.5 deg C/km, resulting in MLCAPE of 1000-1250 J/kg north of I-80. Deep layer shear was 35-40 knots.

Severe thunderstorms produced predominantly damaging winds on this Friday afternoon and evening. An area of showers moved into central Nebraska at mid-afternoon. A lone thunderstorm developed on the southern edge of these showers and briefly became severe over Merrick County, producing hail up to the size of quarters. The main event occurred later in the evening, beginning with several multicell storms that developed from Dawson County west into southern Lincoln County. These storms were initially not severe, but as they headed east, new storms initiated downstream, forming a large cluster of multicell storms. These storms produced multiple reports of strong winds and wind damage over Hall, Hamilton, Clay, York, and Fillmore Counties, mostly along and within 15 miles on either side of Interstate 80. These storms were severe in the 8 to 10 p.m. CST time frame. Tree limbs of various sizes were reported down in multiple locations and winds were measured as high as 62 mph in Grand Island and 59 mph in York and Henderson. There were a couple reports of hail. The largest was the size of ping pong balls in Heartwell, which is in Kearney county.||These storms formed just north of a weak cold front that dropped through most of Nebraska during the morning hours. In the upper levels, a broad ridge encompassed the Plains atop a subtropical high over the Southern Plains. The Central Plains were on the southern fringe of the Westerlies. Surface temperatures in advance of the storms were in the mid 80s, with dewpoints near 70. With mid-level lapse rates of 7.5 deg C/km, MLCAPE values were between 2000 and 3000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was between 30 and 40 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moving quickly through the northern Plains allowed for a seasonably strong cold front to sweep through eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa late in the day on the 9th of August. Isolated to scattered supercells developed along the cold front as it moved southeast across the area with some of the supercells producing severe weather in the form of large hail and damaging winds, as well as locally heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced hail up to the size of golf balls in a few locations on this Tuesday afternoon. Just after 2 p.m. CST, an isolated thunderstorm developed along the Buffalo-Sherman County line. This storm advanced slowly to the east and weakened by 3:30 p.m. By 2:45 p.m., another storm joined it over northern Sherman County. This storm moved north into Valley County by 3:30 p.m. and exited the county by 5 p.m. Both of these storms produced hail up to the size of golf balls. The second storm produced golf ball size hail near Arcadia and near Fort Hartsuff State Park. ||These storms formed well ahead of a backdoor cold front that was pressing into northeastern Nebraska. However, convective initiation was more likely a result of a small area of low-level convergence between Broken Bow, where winds were from the west, and Ord, where winds were from the east. In the upper levels, a subtropical high was over Oklahoma, with a ridge extending north through the Dakotas. Troughs were located over the western and eastern United States. At mid-afternoon, surface temperatures were in the middle 90s, with dewpoints in the middle to upper 60s. With mid-level lapse rates near 7 deg C/km, MLCAPE was around 2500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was near 25 knots.

Between 4-9 p.m. CDT on this Sunday afternoon and evening, a smattering of strong to severe thunderstorms developed along and just ahead of an invading cold front, producing hail in various parts of South Central Nebraska, but especially within Polk, Nance and northeast Merrick counties in far northeast portions of the local area. Within these counties, the main event occurred between 430-630 p.m. CDT as a cluster of semi-discrete severe storms blossomed slightly ahead of the cold front and tracked east-northeast before exiting into eastern Nebraska. Polk County yielded the most hail reports, with nickel to quarter size stones reported in the Osceola and Shelby areas, and larger golf ball size stones reported in western and far northeast sections of the county. In fact, a NeRAIN observer located a few miles south-southwest of Columbus reported broken windows and roof damage in the area. Outside of this main, concentrated zone of severe storms, a combination of linear and small multicell storm structures yielded a few instances of sub-severe hail in the Ord, Red Cloud and Hubbell areas, and a lone quarter size report in Clay Center. By 9 p.m. CDT, the surface cold front had pushed through all of South Central Nebraska, ending all local thunderstorm activity for the night as convection exited Fillmore and Thayer counties to the east. Rainfall-wise, the vast majority of affected areas received well under 1. ||In the mid-upper levels, forcing was provided by the southern reaches of a broad shortwave trough tracking east across the Central and especially Northern Plains, with this trough ushering in increasing west-southwest flow aloft. At the surface, the well-defined, northeast-southwest oriented cold front steadily swept through South Central Nebraska during the afternoon and evening. Ahead of the boundary, afternoon temperatures across most of the local area topped out between 75-80 F as dewpoints rose to between 50-55 F on gusty south winds. By the time storms initiated, the combination of mixed-layer CAPE values on the order of 1000 J/kg and 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-50 knots was plenty sufficient for large hail production.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea sized hail covered the ground, with some quarter sized hail mixed in.

The mid-late afternoon hours on this Tuesday featured a smattering of strong to marginally severe thunderstorms primarily within the southwestern quadrant of South Central Nebraska (generally near/south of Interstate 80 and west of Highway 281). Within this area, there were a handful of severe-criteria wind gust and/or wind damage reports, including estimated 60 MPH gusts with four-inch tree limbs down eight miles south of Elwood, some minor tree damage in Red Cloud and also some limited tree damage and a power line down in Gibbon. There were no reports of large hail. Although there was no ground-truth confirmation of flooding, locally heavy rain in excess of three inches drenched portions of southeastern Harlan and far southwestern Franklin counties due to a combination of not only afternoon convection, but also redevelopment of a few strong storms during the night between 9 p.m. and 3 a.m. CDT. Within this small zone, event-total rain totals featured 3.75 two miles northwest of Republican City (NeRAIN observer) and 3.37 at Naponee (NWS coop observer). ||Focusing on timing, weak convection got underway early in the afternoon within extreme western portions of South Central Nebraska. However, it took a few hours for strong to severe activity initially over southwestern Nebraska to create a sufficient cold pool and push eastward into the local area between 3-4 p.m. CDT in the form of a north-to-south oriented bowing line segment. While this initial surge of winds resulted in the damage reports from Gosper and Buffalo counties, newly developing multicell storms soon blossomed farther east within counties along the state line, including the one responsible for tree damage in Red Cloud. While there were no reports of severe weather after 4:30 p.m. CDT, fairly widespread coverage of showers and storms persisted within much of South Central Nebraska through around 8 p.m. CDT. Beyond sunset, isolated to scattered thunderstorms redeveloped over several state-line counties along the nose of a modest low level jet, producing the aforementioned heavy downpours in Harlan/Franklin counties. Finally, by 6 a.m. CDT, the vast majority of South Central Nebraska was rain-free. In the larger scale of the mid-upper levels, this event was instigated by a slow-moving, elongated shortwave trough gradually sinking from northwest-to-southeast through the Central Plains. At the surface, afternoon convection intensified just to the north of a quasi-stationary front stretched west-to-east across northern Kansas. Around the time severe storms first entered the local area temperatures were only in the 70s F, but the mesoscale environment contained around 1500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and deep layer wind shear of 30-35 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour rainfall total of 3.37 inches occurred in Naponee. Rain began in the early afternoon on August 4th.

Although there was no ground-truth confirmation of flooding, locally heavy rain in excess of three inches drenched portions of southeastern Harlan and far southwestern Franklin counties due to a combination of not only afternoon convection, but also redevelopment of a few strong storms during the night between 9 p.m. and 3 a.m. CDT. Within this small zone, event-total rain totals featured 3.75 two miles northwest of Republican City (NeRAIN observer) and 3.37 at Naponee (NWS coop observer). ||Focusing on timing, weak convection got underway early in the afternoon within extreme western portions of South Central Nebraska. However, it took a few hours for strong to severe activity initially over southwestern Nebraska to create a sufficient cold pool and push eastward into the local area between 3-4 p.m. CDT in the form of a north-to-south oriented bowing line segment. While this initial surge of winds resulted in the damage reports from Gosper and Buffalo counties, newly developing multicell storms soon blossomed farther east within counties along the state line, including the one responsible for tree damage in Red Cloud. While there were no reports of severe weather after 4:30 p.m. CDT, fairly widespread coverage of showers and storms persisted within much of South Central Nebraska through around 8 p.m. CDT. Beyond sunset, isolated to scattered thunderstorms redeveloped over several state-line counties along the nose of a modest low level jet, producing the aforementioned heavy downpours in Harlan/Franklin counties. Finally, by 6 a.m. CDT, the vast majority of South Central Nebraska was rain-free. In the larger scale of the mid-upper levels, this event was instigated by a slow-moving, elongated shortwave trough gradually sinking from northwest-to-southeast through the Central Plains. At the surface, afternoon convection intensified just to the north of a quasi-stationary front stretched west-to-east across northern Kansas. Around the time severe storms first entered the local area temperatures were only in the 70s F, but the mesoscale environment contained around 1500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and deep layer wind shear of 30-35 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple eight-inch diameter tree branches were blown down, and consequently knocked down a power line in Gibbon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed out ahead of a surface trough along a couple of confluence zones in an area with steep mid level lapse rates and limited instability. There were a couple severe hail reports...the largest of which occurred from a splitting cell over Colfax county.

Thunderstorms developed in two areas of eastern Nebraska on the afternoon of May 3rd. The first and most intense developed near Lincoln at the intersection of a surface trough and a thermal boundary, which was the result of earlier day weaker thunderstorms. Although moisture and thus instability were limited, favorable shear and boundary interaction allowed for a couple of supercells to track southeast across southeast Nebraska producing significant hail and one weak tornado. The other area of thunderstorm development was across northeast Nebraska associated with a cold front. This area had limited instability as well, but also weaker shear than farther south and resulted in less intense storms. The threat of severe weather generally ended shortly after midnight as the storms weakened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranged in size at different times from penny size to as large as 2 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several small, discrete supercells developed late in the afternoon on this Wednesday, and continued into the evening. While there were many reports of wall clouds and funnel clouds, these storms did not produce much severe weather. Most storms exhibited classic supercell characteristics on radar, with some supercells splitting. The only confirmed severe weather was 1.5 inch hail in Red Cloud, which is in Webster county, and a measured wind gust of 67 mph near the town of Chester, in southern Thayer county.||These storms formed north of a cold front that moved through the previous night and became stationary over northern Kansas. In the upper-levels, a subtropical high was over Texas, with a ridge extending north to Minnesota. Troughs were along the East Coast and over the western United States. A shortwave trough was embedded, in the western trough, from Montana south to Utah. Surface temperatures were in the lower to middle 80s, with dewpoints in the upper 60s and lower 70s. Mid-level lapse rates were low, resulting in MLCAPE between 1000 and 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 30-35 knots. Low-level (0-3 km) SRH was between 150 and 300 m2/s2. However, 0-1 km shear was less than optimal, between 10 and 15 knots. While this environment was supportive of supercells, the lack of low-level shear may have contributed to the lack of tornadoes.

Severe thunderstorms produced predominantly damaging winds on this Friday afternoon and evening. An area of showers moved into central Nebraska at mid-afternoon. A lone thunderstorm developed on the southern edge of these showers and briefly became severe over Merrick County, producing hail up to the size of quarters. The main event occurred later in the evening, beginning with several multicell storms that developed from Dawson County west into southern Lincoln County. These storms were initially not severe, but as they headed east, new storms initiated downstream, forming a large cluster of multicell storms. These storms produced multiple reports of strong winds and wind damage over Hall, Hamilton, Clay, York, and Fillmore Counties, mostly along and within 15 miles on either side of Interstate 80. These storms were severe in the 8 to 10 p.m. CST time frame. Tree limbs of various sizes were reported down in multiple locations and winds were measured as high as 62 mph in Grand Island and 59 mph in York and Henderson. There were a couple reports of hail. The largest was the size of ping pong balls in Heartwell, which is in Kearney county.||These storms formed just north of a weak cold front that dropped through most of Nebraska during the morning hours. In the upper levels, a broad ridge encompassed the Plains atop a subtropical high over the Southern Plains. The Central Plains were on the southern fringe of the Westerlies. Surface temperatures in advance of the storms were in the mid 80s, with dewpoints near 70. With mid-level lapse rates of 7.5 deg C/km, MLCAPE values were between 2000 and 3000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was between 30 and 40 knots.

These storms were severe in the 8 to 10 p.m. CST time frame. Surface temperatures in advance of the storms were in the mid 80s, with dewpoints near 70. With mid-level lapse rates of 7.5 deg C/km, MLCAPE values were between 2000 and 3000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was between 30 and 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 60 MPH resulted in some minor shingle damage at an area home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Primarily between 4-8 p.m. CDT on this Sunday, several multicell thunderstorms rumbled across various portions of South Central Nebraska, but with the overall-greatest coverage focused within counties north of Interstate 80. While most of these storms produced nothing more than small hail and non-severe wind gusts, one flared up to severe levels over Sherman County mainly between 5:30-6:00 p.m. CDT, yielding a couple quarter size hail reports from the Loup City and Ashton areas. A few miles south of Ashton, one resident reported vinyl siding damage along with a destroyed garden. As the early evening hours wore on, this scattered convection steadily faded away with the loss of daytime heating. ||In the most basic sense, this largely diurnally-driven convection was sparked by a combination of strong surface heating and high instability near a weak, west-east oriented cold front sagging down from northern Nebraska, along with limited support aloft from a subtle disturbance embedded within broad northwesterly flow. Despite somewhat warm mid level temperatures, the combination of afternoon highs rising well into the 90s F and dewpoints averaging in the mid-60s to low-70s helped promote considerable instability with mixed-layer CAPE on the order of 2000-4000 J/kg. Storm organization was likely limited somewhat by only modest deep-layer wind shear generally on the order of 30-35 knots.

This Sunday afternoon and evening featured a rather localized-but-significant flooding event within primarily central and northeast portions of Furnas County, including the community of Edison, which was ground zero for flooding rains and also a brief bout of severe wind gusts estimated up to 65 MPH, along with hail up to quarter size. According to a trained spotter two miles southwest of Edison, an unofficial rain total of 5 inches poured down in a 90-minute span between 3:30-5:00 p.m. CDT, washing out part of a driveway. In Edison itself, the NWS cooperative observer recorded a slightly lower storm total of 3.74, but this was still more than enough rain in a short time to cause significant flooding of several roads and some structures. Some of the worst damage occurred at Ag Valley Coop, where a shallow depth of water infiltrated much of the first floor office. Even after the last drops of lingering light rain vacated Edison around 9 p.m. CDT, impactful flooding persisted well into the morning hours of the 17th. Although the Furnas County flooding situation was the big story from this event, radar data suggested that other pockets of flooding might have also occurred in portions of eastern Gosper and western Phelps counties, where rain totals of at least 2-3 were also fairly common. ||For the vast majority of the 16th, all the way from the pre-dawn hours through midnight, at least isolated to scattered showers and mainly weak thunderstorms affected at least some portion of South Central Nebraska, primarily within counties northwest of line from Franklin to York. While most of this convection was fairly disorganized and weak, the mid-afternoon through early evening hours featured a noticeable uptick in storm coverage and intensity, especially in southwestern counties such as Furnas, Gosper, and Phelps. Within this area, an initially weak cluster of convection rapidly intensified between 3-6 p.m. CDT, morphing into a nearly solid, southward-sinking line of slow-moving strong to severe storms. The primary synoptic scale trigger for this event was a very slow-moving, southwest-to-northeast oriented surface cold front roughly bisecting Nebraska during the afternoon and evening. Considerable moisture pooled in the vicinity of this boundary, featuring surface dewpoints in the mid-60s to low-70s F and precipitable water values averaging 1.50-2.00. In the mid-upper levels, forcing was rather subtle and weak, with southern Nebraska positioned well-south of a primary shortwave trough shifting from the Northern Rockies into the Dakotas. Around the time of the most intense Furnas County convection, mesoscale parameters featured at least 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE, but weak deep-layer wind shear only around 25 knots, helping promote slow storm motions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads were under water in and around the Edison area due to flash flooding. In Edison, Ag Valley Coop sustained some of the worst damage as flood waters infiltrated the first floor office. Other homes and buildings in and around Edison were also flooded. As of 4:25 pm CDT, 4.25 inches of rain was reported to have fallen two miles southwest of Edison (fell in one approximately one hour) and washed a dip in a driveway 2 feet deep. By 5 p.m. CDT, rainfall at that location was unofficially up to 5. In addition, the NWS cooperative observer in Edison reported a storm total of 3.74.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite a fairly favorable setup for severe thunderstorms on this Thursday afternoon and evening, the ingredients didn't quite come together across the vast majority of South Central Nebraska, with radar data suggesting only the presence of a few strong storms roaming counties along and north of Interstate 80. However, a delayed storm report received the next day revealed that a tiny fraction of the area did in fact experience a severe wind event: a Merrick County farmer reported a flipped irrigation pivot and significant crop damage in a localized area near Silver Creek. Although radar data was inconclusive regarding the severity of these winds (due in part to the  lowest beam height situated 6,000 feet above ground level at that distance), a fairly strong reflectivity core did pass through that area around 9 p.m. CDT within an environment featuring strong low level wind shear (see below). A few hours prior to the Merrick County damage, northwestern portions of South Central Nebraska narrowly avoided a severe weather threat from a cluster of splitting supercells in Custer County. However, these storms weakened before entering local counties such as Dawson and Valley. Then, between 8-10 p.m. CDT, a handful of strong multicell storms flared up within the northeast quadrant of South Central Nebraska in response to a strengthening low level jet, including the Merrick County storm. While additional non-severe storms lingered over northeast counties such as Nance, Merrick and Polk past the midnight hour, all of South Central Nebraska was storm-free by 2:30 a.m. CDT.||This was a somewhat unusual severe weather setup by late-August standards, as a seasonably well-defined surface low and attendant eastward-arcing warm front became established over the heart of South Central Nebraska during the afternoon and evening. Although mid/upper level flow wasn't overly strong, a fairly compact vort max tracked over central Nebraska, well to the north of an expansive ridge anchored over northern Mexico. In addition, a seasonably-strong southerly low level jet ramped up to around 40 knots over much of central and eastern Nebraska by early evening. The combination of this low level jet and the underlying surface warm front yielded an impressive 30 knots of 0-1 kilometer low level wind shear around the time of the Merrick County wind damage. Other early-evening mesoscale parameters were more modest, including roughly 1000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE and 30 knots of deep-layer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated near 70 MPH blew over an irrigation pivot and resulted in significant crop damage within a localized area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly significant-but-localized rural flooding event unfolded within north central and northeast portions of Polk County, ramping up during the early morning hours of Tuesday Aug. 18th and persisting into the afternoon of Wednesday the 19th before markedly improving (severe weather in the form of large hail/damaging winds were not an issue). The worst flooding impacts were confined to a rather narrow, roughly 5-8 mile wide corridor extending west-to-east several miles north of Osceola and Shelby, where generally 6-10 of rain accumulated over the course of roughly 36 hours (see below for timing details). Within this zone, county officials reported that 25-30 gravel roads were under water or closed, with a few completely washed out. In addition, Clear Creek flooded within northeastern portions of the county, as the gauge 10 miles north of Shelby crested just above the minor flood stage of 8 ft. on the morning of the 19th. Although ground-truth, event-total reports were limited within the narrow corridor of maximum rainfall, a NeRAIN observer seven miles north of Osceola measured 6.69, and an unofficial public gauge six miles south of Duncan captured 8.50. Another nearby NeRAIN observer commented that this was the worst road flooding they had observed within the local area in at least 63 years. ||This was a somewhat unique flooding event considering that it did not occur solely as a result of one significant rainfall. Instead, it stemmed from the cumulative effect of primarily three rounds of heavy rain over the course of roughly 36 hours, with the third and final round serving as the proverbial final straw, resulting in the impactful flooding. In the mid-upper levels, broad quasi-zonal flow was in place over Nebraska during most of this time, before a larger scale shortwave trough finally passed through on the 18th, sweeping rain chances out of the local area. The first concentrated round of heavy rain moved through the county on the evening of Sunday the 16th, part of a larger complex of showers and thunderstorms focused near a slow-moving cold front. Following a rather short lull, the second primary round of rain flared up during the morning hours of Monday the 17th. Combined, these first two rounds dropped generally 2-4, but not enough to instigate flooding. Although a few spotty showers affected the area during the afternoon and early evening of the 17th, this was largely a dry period before a strengthening nocturnal low level jet sparked a narrow axis of persistent heavy rain over northern Polk County mainly between 9:30 p.m. and 12:30 a.m. CDT. This third round, which soaked the area with another 2-5, was clearly the one that prompted significant flooding issues, although one final burst of rain also passed through a few hours later around sunrise on the 18th. Fortunately, a solid stretch of dry weather returned to Polk County thereafter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads were flooded. Around 25 to 30 county roads (many were gravel roads) were closed in north central and northeastern portions of Polk County. At least one paved road had water running over it a few miles west of the Highway 81 and 64 junction. Some gravel roads were washed out in spots. Gauge readings and radar estimation indicated a narrow swath of six to 10 inches of rain fell in separate rounds over the course of roughly 30 hours. Minor flooding occurred along Clear Creek within northeast portions of the county. An NeRAIN observer seven miles north of Osceola reported 6.69 inches of rain between the evening of August 16th and the early morning of August 18th. The majority of the rain fell between 930 p.m. CDT August 17th through 1230 a.m. August 18th. Also, an unofficial public report of 8.50 of storm total rain occurred six miles south of Duncan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly significant-but-localized rural flooding event unfolded within north central and northeast portions of Polk County, ramping up during the early morning hours of Tuesday Aug. 18th and persisting into the afternoon of Wednesday the 19th before markedly improving (severe weather in the form of large hail/damaging winds were not an issue). The worst flooding impacts were confined to a rather narrow, roughly 5-8 mile wide corridor extending west-to-east several miles north of Osceola and Shelby, where generally 6-10 of rain accumulated over the course of roughly 36 hours (see below for timing details). Although a few spotty showers affected the area during the afternoon and early evening of the 17th, this was largely a dry period before a strengthening nocturnal low level jet sparked a narrow axis of persistent heavy rain over northern Polk County mainly between 9:30 p.m. and 12:30 a.m. CDT.
