Fortunately, a solid stretch of dry weather returned to Polk County thereafter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 25 to 30 county roads (many were gravel roads) were closed due to standing water in north central and northeastern portions of Polk County. At least one paved road had water running over it a few miles west of the Highway 81 and 64 junction. Some gravel roads were washed out in spots. Gauge readings and radar estimation indicated a narrow swath of six to 10 inches of rain fell in separate rounds over the course of roughly 30 hours. Minor flooding occurred along Clear Creek within northeast portions of the county. An NeRAIN observer seven miles north of Osceola reported 6.69 inches of rain between the evening of August 16th and the early morning of August 18th. The majority of the rain fell between 930 p.m. CDT August 17th through 1230 a.m. August 18th. Also, an unofficial public report of 8.50 of storm total rain occurred six miles south of Duncan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Evening thunderstorms over portions of the northwest Nebraska Panhandle produced  large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed three miles northwest of the Chadron Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours in Sheridan County and moved southward through the eastern panhandle and portions of southwest Nebraska during the evening hours. Isolated thunderstorms also occurred in Blaine County of north central Nebraska during the early evening hours producing damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in central Cherry County and moved southeast producing large hail, heavy rain, and gusty winds. Other thunderstorms developed in the southeast Nebraska Panhandle and southwest that also produced large hail, damaging winds, and heavy rain. The heavy rainfall led to localized flash flooding in portions of Keith, Perkins, Lincoln, and Hayes counties.
Slow moving, severe thunderstorms developed producing heavy rain and large hail across Chase and Hayes counties during the evening of July 25th. Hail to teacup size fell.

This was a rather active weather night for South Central Nebraska, highlighted by a handful of severe-criteria wind reports within counties along and south of Interstate 80. Although widespread precipitation affected the area for several hours on this Friday night into early Saturday morning, all severe wind reports occurred between 11:30 p.m. and 12:30 a.m. CDT as a north-to-south oriented bowing segment charged through. Along and near the leading edge of the surging outflow, wind gusts around 60 MPH were reported near Gibbon, in rural southeastern Adams and southwestern Clay counties, and also near Superior. Well-behind this bowing line, severe gusts associated with a well-defined mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) brought down numerous tree limbs in Cozad. Although there were no confirmed tornadoes, the presence of seasonably-strong low level shear aided the development of a few brief, radar-indicated rotational signatures, one of which prompted a short-fused Tornado Warning for southern Adams County. Rainfall-wise, though many areas roughly southwest of a Ravenna-Hastings-Hebron line picked up 1-2, the fairly progressive nature of the heaviest cores held flooding issues to a minimum, save for typical short-term street flooding in communities such as Elm Creek. ||Timing-wise, most of South Central Nebraska remained rain-free until after dark, save for a narrow band of weak convection that lifted through several northern and eastern counties during the early evening. However, a pair of expanding severe storm clusters gradually took aim on the local area as they approached from southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas, eventually merging into an organized mesoscale convective system (MCS) that surged into western portions of South Central Nebraska between 10-11 p.m. CDT. By the midnight to 1 a.m. CDT time frame, the primary bowing segment raced through counties south of Interstate 80 and east of Highway 281 before departing into southeast Nebraska and northeast Kansas. In its wake, a large shield of light to moderate rain persisted across the local area for most of the night. In the mid/upper levels, this event featured slightly-stronger-than-average forcing by early August standards, as a well-defined lead disturbance moved out of the Central Rockies into the Central Plains in quasi-zonal flow aloft, downstream from a larger scale parent trough centered over Nevada. By the time the MCS entered the local area, convection was being sustained/enhanced by a 30-40 knot southerly low level jet evident at 850 millibars. At the surface, South Central Nebraska featured a light easterly flow regime, to the north of a quasi-stationary front loosely draped across the southern half of Kansas. Around the time of the severe wind reports, mesoscale parameters featured 1000-2000 J/kg most unstable CAPE, deep layer wind shear of 30-40 knots and rather strong 0-1 kilometer low level shear of 20-30 knots.

In what had already been a very active start to June across parts of South Central Nebraska, what transpired on the afternoon of Wednesday the 10th into the early morning of Thursday the 11th was the final, truly widespread severe weather/flooding event of the month before a noticeably quieter weather pattern settled in over the next few weeks. Focusing first on the severe weather aspect, the afternoon and evening featured several reports of large hail and damaging winds within various counties. Storm mode was primarily multicell, some of which started out as semi-discrete before eventually merging into larger clusters and lines. Most hail reports were in the nickel to half dollar size range, with 1.25 diameter stones reported near Elwood and Lexington. However, one storm that tracked north of Lexington around 6 p.m. CDT was a prolific hail-producer, as a storm chaser reported a short stretch of Highway 21 completely hail-covered with most stones averaging golf ball size but one as large as 3 in diameter. As for damaging winds, there were primarily three noteworthy storms. First off, a cluster of severe storms shifted eastward into the Valley/Sherman counties area between 5:30-6:30 p.m. CDT. In Arcadia, the NWS cooperative observer estimated 70 MPH gusts, with 10-12 diameter tree limbs down. A bit later, a storm became severe near the Franklin/Kearney County line around 7 p.m. CDT and rapidly tracked northeast during the next few hours. Along the way, estimated 70 MPH winds overturned seven empty freight cars of a 72-car train near Kenesaw in extreme western Adams County. This storm weakened before reaching Grand Island, with only 50-60 MPH gusts reported on the south side of town around 8:30 p.m. CDT. While the train-toppling storm was in progress, a separate severe storm to its northeast passed through Merrick and northern Hamilton counties, causing tree and power line damage in Central City and Hordville around 8 p.m. CDT. By 9 p.m. CDT, storms had largely settled below severe-limits, with just one additional 60 MPH wind report along the Franklin/Webster County line. Through the remainder of the night, scattered to numerous showers and non-severe storms persisted over much of South Central Nebraska, with most counties finally rain-free by sunrise. As for rainfall amounts, the majority of the 24-county area totaled 1-2. However, there were a few smaller pockets of at least 2-3, most notably within York, northern Hamilton and southwestern Polk counties, where NeRAIN observers reported 3.46 near Hordville and 3.24 near Benedict.  Although a few dry days leading up to this event had allowed the York County area to start drying out in the wake of flooding rains that fell primarily on the 4th-5th, this event resulted in renewed short-term flooding issues. Most notably this involved the West Fork Big Blue River near McCool Junction, which again came out of its banks during the day on the 11th, only a few days after historic flooding occurred. Fortunately, flooding was not as severe this time around, and the river largely fell back into its banks by the end of the day on Friday the 12th (please refer to the June 4th p.m.-June 5th a.m. episode narrative for much more detail regarding multiple days of flooding along the West Fork Big Blue River). ||Most of the timing details of this severe weather event were already covered in the preceding paragraph, as all hail/strong wind reports occurred between 4:00-9:30 p.m. CDT. However, the first strong storms of the afternoon actually developed eastward into the Dawson/Gosper counties area as early as 2:30-4:00 p.m. CDT. Turning to the synoptic and mesoscale setup: the primary trigger aloft was a series of fairly subtle disturbances gradually overspreading the Central Plains, out ahead of a modest shortwave trough drifting southeast toward the region from the Northern Rockies. At the surface, a few different boundaries helped to focus convection, including a generally west-to-east oriented quasi-stationary front draped through central Nebraska and also a trough axis extending southwest from the local area to weak low pressure centered near the KS/CO border. With afternoon temperatures rising between 90-95 degrees, storms erupted in an environment featuring around 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 30-40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear. During the night, widespread weak convection persisted within the exit region of a 30-40 knot low level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over Banner and Kimball counties produced several funnel clouds, damaging winds and torrential rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two funnel clouds were sighted 11 miles northwest of the Kimball Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front across western Nebraska and northeastern Colorado during the late afternoon hours on July 18th. As this activity moved east it became severe across the western Nebraska Sandhills and southwestern Nebraska. Damaging winds impacted crops and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported thunderstorm wind gusts around 70 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms over Kimball and Cheyenne counties produced torrential rainfall that resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of rain in two hours produced flash flooding along Lodgepole Creek and US Highway 30 from east of Potter through Brownson to just west of Sidney.  Extensive lowland flooding was reported north and south of Interstate 80.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed over the Nebraska Panhandle during the early evening hours of July 27th.  They developed in the vicinity of a surface trough of low pressure, and moved to the east northeast into the western Nebraska Sandhills and portions of the northeastern Nebraska Panhandle. Severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust of 60 MPH measured with a Davis instrument.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous, semi-discrete and multicell thunderstorms blossomed across much of South Central Nebraska on this Saturday afternoon and evening, with a few storms becoming severe and producing hail ranging from 1-2 in diameter. The first severe storm of the afternoon developed near Kearney around 5 p.m. CDT, and was likely the most prolific hail-producer as it slowly drifted north-northwest through central and northwest Buffalo County for nearly two hours. Just north of Kearney, a few vehicles slid off of Highway 10 as it became hail-covered and slick. This storm also produced the largest reported hail of the day (2) north of Riverdale. Between 6-9 p.m. CDT, several other storms either flared up over various parts of the area or pushed northward out of Kansas, with quarter size hail reported near the state line south of Red Cloud and also in Wood River. Even though the mesoscale environment was potentially supportive of a somewhat greater severe weather threat, the fairly rapid development of plentiful convection and resultant storm-scale outflow interference likely mitigated this. With the loss of diurnal heating, storms steadily weakened and diminished in coverage within a few hours after nightfall. Although most affected areas received less than 1 of rainfall, much of northern Kearney County along with adjacent portions of southeast Buffalo, southwest Hall and northwest Adams counties received higher amounts to around 2. ||In the mid-upper levels, the primary player was an expansive closed low churning slowly eastward through eastern Colorado, with several smaller-scale disturbances shifting northward into the Central Plains along its eastern flank. At the surface, there were no distinct synoptic features within South Central Nebraska, only fairly weak, broad low pressure and prevailing light southerly to easterly breezes. Despite afternoon temperatures only rising into the low-70s F, the combination of surface dewpoints in the low to mid-50s and notably cold air aloft (evidenced by 500 millibar temps around -18 C) yielded surface-based CAPE around 1000 J/kg in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of 30-40 knots.

Thunderstorms developed in central Cherry County and moved southeast producing large hail, heavy rain, and gusty winds. Other thunderstorms developed in the southeast Nebraska Panhandle and southwest that also produced large hail, damaging winds, and heavy rain. The heavy rainfall led to localized flash flooding in portions of Keith, Perkins, Lincoln, and Hayes counties.

Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon of the 25th as modified gulf moisture returned north into the middle Missouri River Valley. As moisture returned to the area, instability increased and allowed for the thunderstorm development. The majority of the thunderstorms were non-severe in nature due to the weak atmospheric shear and limited instability, but isolated large hail was observed in southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.
Late afternoon and early evening thunderstorms produced strong winds and large hail across portions of the western Nebraska Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 mph were observed on the west side of Minatare.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon thunderstorms over the northwest Nebraska Panhandle produced several reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed 20 miles north of Harrison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed early this Wednesday afternoon and evening, and predominantly produced tornadoes and flash flooding. With no cap in place, scattered thunderstorms began developing as early as the noon hour over north central Kansas. Most storms rapidly became supercellular. These supercells rapidly lifted north into south central Nebraska and began producing severe weather by 330 p.m. CDT. Most of the tornadoes occurred over Nuckolls and Thayer counties. One tornado did occur over Adams county and a brief spin-up also occurred over Hall county. Eventually, an MCS developed. The leading edge of the cold pool becoming stationary. New storms repeatedly developed over north central Kansas and lifted northeast along the boundary, through Nuckolls and Thayer counties and further downstream into southeastern Nebraska. Cell training produced a swath of 5-11 inches of rain over portions of Nuckolls and Thayer counties. The highest amount reported was 10.91 inches just west of Hebron. This amount of rainfall in 24 hours typically occurs once in 1,000 years at any given location, or stated another way, has a .1% chance of occurrence in a given 24 hour period. Cooperative observers in Hebron and Superior reported 8.59 and 7.13 inches, respectively. These were the highest 24-hour rainfall amounts ever recorded at these locations, dating back to 1893. Flash flooding damaged roads and some bridges were washed out. U.S. Highways 81 and 136 were closed for a time due to the flooding.||A complex low pressure system extended from the Canadian Prairies into the western United States. These storms developed in the warm sector, far away from any discernible surface boundaries. However, an axis of confluence was present over north central Kansas. A deep upper-level trough was over the western U.S., with a ridge over the East. In the southwest flow between these features, a shortwave trough was lifting through the Central Plains. These storms formed in the southeast quadrant of the associated vorticity maximum. Around the time thunderstorms developed, temperatures were in the lower 70s with dewpoints in the lower 60s, resulting in low cloud bases. Even at mid-afternoon, cloud bases were only 2000-2500 feet. The surface conditions and mid-level lapse rates of 7 degrees C/km resulted in SBCAPE around 1500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was only 25-30 knots. However, low-level wind fields were strong with 40-45 knots at 850 mb. This resulted in large hodographs with 0-1 km SRH values of 200-250 m2/s2, and 0-3 km SRH of 250-300 m2/s2. Combined with low cloud bases, this created a favorable environment for supercells and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down about 3 miles south of Roseland, clipping the east side of town before lifting just north-northeast of town. This tornado was rated an EF1, with an estimated peak wind speed of 110 MPH. The most widespread damage from this tornado was on the east side of town, and while several homes and outbuildings sustained damage, 2 sustained considerable damage. One of those homes slide from its foundation and another lost its entire roof structure. A large metal building with wood post frame construction was destroyed and tree damage was widespread throughout town. The tornado traveled just a few hundred feet east of Silver Lake School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In what had already been a very active start to June across parts of South Central Nebraska, what transpired on the afternoon of Wednesday the 10th into the early morning of Thursday the 11th was the final, truly widespread severe weather/flooding event of the month before a noticeably quieter weather pattern settled in over the next few weeks. Focusing first on the severe weather aspect, the afternoon and evening featured several reports of large hail and damaging winds within various counties. Storm mode was primarily multicell, some of which started out as semi-discrete before eventually merging into larger clusters and lines. Most hail reports were in the nickel to half dollar size range, with 1.25 diameter stones reported near Elwood and Lexington. However, one storm that tracked north of Lexington around 6 p.m. CDT was a prolific hail-producer, as a storm chaser reported a short stretch of Highway 21 completely hail-covered with most stones averaging golf ball size but one as large as 3 in diameter. As for damaging winds, there were primarily three noteworthy storms. First off, a cluster of severe storms shifted eastward into the Valley/Sherman counties area between 5:30-6:30 p.m. CDT. In Arcadia, the NWS cooperative observer estimated 70 MPH gusts, with 10-12 diameter tree limbs down. A bit later, a storm became severe near the Franklin/Kearney County line around 7 p.m. CDT and rapidly tracked northeast during the next few hours. Along the way, estimated 70 MPH winds overturned seven empty freight cars of a 72-car train near Kenesaw in extreme western Adams County. This storm weakened before reaching Grand Island, with only 50-60 MPH gusts reported on the south side of town around 8:30 p.m. CDT. While the train-toppling storm was in progress, a separate severe storm to its northeast passed through Merrick and northern Hamilton counties, causing tree and power line damage in Central City and Hordville around 8 p.m. CDT. By 9 p.m. CDT, storms had largely settled below severe-limits, with just one additional 60 MPH wind report along the Franklin/Webster County line. Through the remainder of the night, scattered to numerous showers and non-severe storms persisted over much of South Central Nebraska, with most counties finally rain-free by sunrise. As for rainfall amounts, the majority of the 24-county area totaled 1-2. However, there were a few smaller pockets of at least 2-3, most notably within York, northern Hamilton and southwestern Polk counties, where NeRAIN observers reported 3.46 near Hordville and 3.24 near Benedict.  Although a few dry days leading up to this event had allowed the York County area to start drying out in the wake of flooding rains that fell primarily on the 4th-5th, this event resulted in renewed short-term flooding issues. Most notably this involved the West Fork Big Blue River near McCool Junction, which again came out of its banks during the day on the 11th, only a few days after historic flooding occurred. Fortunately, flooding was not as severe this time around, and the river largely fell back into its banks by the end of the day on Friday the 12th (please refer to the June 4th p.m.-June 5th a.m. episode narrative for much more detail regarding multiple days of flooding along the West Fork Big Blue River). ||Most of the timing details of this severe weather event were already covered in the preceding paragraph, as all hail/strong wind reports occurred between 4:00-9:30 p.m. CDT. However, the first strong storms of the afternoon actually developed eastward into the Dawson/Gosper counties area as early as 2:30-4:00 p.m. CDT. Turning to the synoptic and mesoscale setup: the primary trigger aloft was a series of fairly subtle disturbances gradually overspreading the Central Plains, out ahead of a modest shortwave trough drifting southeast toward the region from the Northern Rockies. At the surface, a few different boundaries helped to focus convection, including a generally west-to-east oriented quasi-stationary front draped through central Nebraska and also a trough axis extending southwest from the local area to weak low pressure centered near the KS/CO border. With afternoon temperatures rising between 90-95 degrees, storms erupted in an environment featuring around 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 30-40 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear. During the night, widespread weak convection persisted within the exit region of a 30-40 knot low level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small stretch of Highway 21 was completely covered in hail. Most hail stones were near the size of golf balls, with some as large as 3 in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front across western Nebraska and northeastern Colorado during the late afternoon hours on July 18th. As this activity moved east it became severe across the western Nebraska Sandhills and southwestern Nebraska. Damaging winds impacted crops and power lines.

A cold front moved into eastern Nebraska during the afternoon of the 1st. Ahead of the cold front some moisture return from the southern Pains coupled with steep lapse-rates aloft to create marginal instability. Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed along the cold front by late afternoon, developing into a line of thunderstorms before moving southeast through eastern Nebraska and into western Iowa. Isolated severe weather was reported along this line.

Thunderstorms developed in two areas of eastern Nebraska on the afternoon of May 3rd. The first and most intense developed near Lincoln at the intersection of a surface trough and a thermal boundary, which was the result of earlier day weaker thunderstorms. Although moisture and thus instability were limited, favorable shear and boundary interaction allowed for a couple of supercells to track southeast across southeast Nebraska producing significant hail and one weak tornado. The other area of thunderstorm development was across northeast Nebraska associated with a cold front. This area had limited instability as well, but also weaker shear than farther south and resulted in less intense storms. The threat of severe weather generally ended shortly after midnight as the storms weakened.

Strong to severe thunderstorms developed over portions of north central Nebraska in association with a surface trough of low pressure across the Nebraska Panhandle. A second area of strong to severe thunderstorms formed across southwestern Nebraska, along and north of a warm front which was draped over far southwestern Nebraska.

Strong to severe thunderstorms developed over portions of north central Nebraska in association with a surface trough of low pressure across the Nebraska Panhandle. A second area of strong to severe thunderstorms formed across southwestern Nebraska, along and north of a warm front which was draped over far southwestern Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust to 60 MPH reported at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the Nebraska panhandle and northeast Colorado during the afternoon. The storms intensified and expanded as they moved into southwest Nebraska, producing severe wind gusts and large hail. Training storms over Chase and Hayes counties led to rainfall amounts up to 3 in spots and localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were rendered impassible near Hayes Center due to heavy rain and washouts in spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved over northern Red Willow County during the evening producing hail up to tennis ball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hailstones were as large as tennis balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous strong to occasionally severe multicell storms blossomed across South Central Nebraska from the late evening of Wednesday the 3rd into the morning hours of Thursday the 4th, serving as round 1 of an active few days of severe storms and flooding rainfall. With storms ranging in character from semi-discrete to clusters and line segments, they primarily produced hail up to around quarter size, along with heavy rain. Although convection affected parts of several counties at some point during the night, the majority of ground-truth severe weather reports emanated from Adams and Clay counties, including quarter size hail on the east side of Hastings, hail up to golf ball size in Clay Center, and several hours later, a combination of quarter size hail and estimated 60 MPH winds six miles east-southeast of Clay Center. Rainfall-wise, the big story of the night was a concentrated swath of generally 1.50-3.00 that fell over southern Hall/northern Adams counties, southeastward through most of Clay, Fillmore and northern Thayer counties. Within this corridor, a few of the highest overnight totals from NeRAIN observers included 3.13 near Alexandria and 3.00 a few miles northwest of Hastings. As a result, minor flooding affected several creeks and streams and some roadways. In fact, the southbound lanes of Highway 281 in far-southern Hall County were closed for a few hours during the night due to flooding. Although this generally minor flooding subsided during the day of the 4th, unfortunately many of these same areas would soon be drenched with another 2-4 on the evening of the 4th into the morning of the 5th, resulting in 36-hour totals of 3-7 and a more serious, widespread flooding situation (please refer to the separate June 4th p.m.-June 5th a.m. severe weather episode for further details). ||Breaking down the overall event timing, all convection through the pre-dark hours focused to the south over northern Kansas. Then, between 10 p.m.-midnight CDT, storms began developing northward into the southern two rows of South Central Nebraska counties, largely in response to a strengthening, northward-lifting low level jet. Over the course of the next several hours, numerous strong to occasionally severe storms continued spreading north and east across the area, with half dollar size hail reported as far north as southern Greeley County. Within the aforementioned primary zone of heavy rain, the majority fell between 2:30-6:00 a.m. CDT. As one area of storms was in the process of exiting eastern counties of the local area around sunrise, a separate, quick-moving line of non-severe convection moved in from the southwest, sweeping across most of South Central Nebraska between 5-9 a.m. CDT. Although a few storms persisted here and there through late-morning, most places began a brief respite before the next round of evening/overnight storms arrived. Turning to the synoptic and mesoscale situation: with a generally west-east oriented surface front residing near the Nebraska-Kansas border through the night, seasonably-strong instability and shear in place, and a strengthening 30-40 knot southerly low level jet taking direct aim on the local area, this was a well-anticipated nocturnal convective flare-up. On the large scale, the Central Plains was under broad west-southwest flow aloft, well-downstream from a large trough centered over the West Coast. At the time of the first severe-criteria hail reports around 11:30 p.m. CDT, the environment featured most-unstable CAPE of 1000-2000 J/kg and 0-6 kilometer deep-layer wind shear of 30-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter size hail occurred at Dove Hill Avenue and Highway 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved near McCook which produced nickel size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Relayed through Spotter Network.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of upper level low pressure slowly moved across the central Plains during the September 22nd through the 23rd time frame. Significant moisture was in place across the mid Missouri River Valley ahead of this system. This combination led to scattered to numerous thunderstorms over eastern Nebraska and western Iowa starting on the 22nd and continuing into the day on the 23rd. The heavy persistent thunderstorms, especially late in the night and into the early morning hours on the 23rd led to significant flash flooding across parts of western Iowa, specifically in Council Bluffs.

Moisture pooled along a stalled frontal boundary near the Kansas and Nebraska border. The moisture combined with steep lapse rates to create moderate instability along this boundary during the afternoon and evening hours. Scattered thunderstorms developed along this boundary by mid afternoon and continued into the evening hours as an upper level disturbance moved into the central Plains. One storm in particular moved along the Kansas and Nebraska border and produced sporadic significant severe in the form of very large hail and damaging winds.

This was the last of five significant severe hail events this month, in terms of size and the number of reports. Each of these events contained at least one report of 2 inch hail or greater. The uniqueness of this event was that it occurred during the morning hours on this Friday. Around 5 a.m. CST, scattered showers began to develop across much of south central Nebraska. Over the following couple hours, they increased in coverage and strengthened into thunderstorms. By 7:30 a.m. CST, several strong to severe storms were along and north of Interstate 80 from Lexington to Grand Island to Osceola. A couple of them became supercells. One supercell in particular produced most of the hail as it tracked from northern Adams County into southern Hamilton and York counties. Tennis ball to baseball size hail occurred near from near Hansen to Giltner to Trumbull. By 10 a.m. CST, it was all over. The storms hail had exited into eastern Nebraska.||South central Nebraska was in the cold sector. A cold front extended from the Great Lakes southwest across Kansas. High pressure was over the Northern Plains, while a low pressure center was making its way along the front over Kansas. In the upper-levels, a broad trough extended from the Northern Rockies into the Northern Plains. A fairly potent shortwave trough was embedded within this cyclonic flow, just west of the region, and extended from Montana into Wyoming. These storms formed in an area of mid-level warm air advection around 700 mb and frontogenesis between 850 mb and 700 mb. Meanwhile, under the frontal surface, cold air advection was occurring with surface temperatures in the 50s. MUCAPE was between 500 and 1000 J/kg, and deep layer shear was around 40 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very small area of wind damage occurred during the predawn hours on this Friday morning. Shortly after midnight, scattered showers and generally weak thunderstorms developed over south central Nebraska. Most of this activity occurred east of Highway 281, between 2 a.m. and 5 a.m. CST. The small storm that produced this damage moved into southwestern Hamilton County from northwestern Clay County shortly before 3 am CST. It was at 3 a.m. that a very small downburst occurred just southwest and west of Giltner. These were outflow winds immediately ahead of the storm. Grain bins were damaged as a result, with two having roofs pushed in. Another grain bin in a different but nearby location was removed from its location and deposited in a field. This downburst was very brief. Radar indicated strong outflow winds of 50 knots at the lowest elevation angle, at the time of the event, and there was no indication of these winds 5 minutes before or 5 minutes after.||These storms developed in an area of warm air advection associated with a 45 knot low-level jet, in the warm sector of low pressure over the Dakotas. A lee-side trough extended from this low across western Nebraska and Kansas. The flow aloft was moderately amplified with a ridge over the Plains and a trough along the West Coast. Mid-level winds were very weak over Nebraska, on the order of 5-10 knots. Surface temperatures were in the lower to middle 70s, with dewpoints in the 60s. Mid-level lapse rates were steep, nearly 8 deg C/km, resulting in MUCAPE around 1000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was a little less than 20 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 60 MPH resulted in roof damage to a few grain bins west of Giltner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail at least the size of tennis balls fell over Thayer County on this Labor Day Monday. Scattered elevated showers, and a few embedded, weak thunderstorms, progressed through south central Nebraska during the morning and afternoon hours. Much of the area was cloudy through early afternoon, as low stratus lingered from the nighttime hours. Between noon and 1 p.m. CST, the southernmost thunderstorm emerged into Webster County. This storm rapidly intensified and became an isolated, classic supercell as it emerged from an area of cloudiness into sunshine and moved crossed Nuckolls and Thayer Counties. This area was the only portion of south central Nebraska that had seen prolonged sunshine. This storm produced very large hail over Thayer County, and those reports were on the fringe of the most intense core. Given the impressive radar signatures, hail larger than tennis balls may have fallen from west-to-east across central Thayer County. However, the core tracked just north of Highway 136, where population is extremely low. As the storm skirted to the north of Hebron, some wind damage occurred associated with the rear flank downdraft. This storm occurred early in the afternoon and exited Thayer county by 230 p.m. CST. Between 4 and 9 p.m. CST, other storms developed over south central Nebraska. While most were not severe, one storm that developed over Adams County produced hail up to the size of golf balls in Hastings.||South central Nebraska was behind a weak cold front that had moved through Sunday. As the day progressed, this front appeared to lift north back into Nebraska, as differential heating occurred between the cloudy and sunny areas. The equipment at the Hebron airport measured temperatures in the upper 80s and dewpoints in the lower 70s within the storm inflow. Combined with mid-level lapse rates between 7 and 7.5 deg C/km, the resulting MLCAPE was around 2000 J/kg. 0-3 km SRH was between 200 and 300 m2/s2, with deep layer shear 45 to 50 knots. Shear and instability were similar into the evening hours, even though temperatures had cooled into the middle 70s. In the upper-levels, a low was over the Canadian Prairies with a broad trough underneath over the Northern Rockies and Northern Plains. Nebraska was on the southern fringe of the Westerlies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 65 MPH resulted in tree damage in Hebron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the last of five significant severe hail events this month, in terms of size and the number of reports. Each of these events contained at least one report of 2 inch hail or greater. The uniqueness of this event was that it occurred during the morning hours on this Friday. Around 5 a.m. CST, scattered showers began to develop across much of south central Nebraska. Over the following couple hours, they increased in coverage and strengthened into thunderstorms. By 7:30 a.m. CST, several strong to severe storms were along and north of Interstate 80 from Lexington to Grand Island to Osceola. A couple of them became supercells. One supercell in particular produced most of the hail as it tracked from northern Adams County into southern Hamilton and York counties. Tennis ball to baseball size hail occurred near from near Hansen to Giltner to Trumbull. By 10 a.m. CST, it was all over. The storms hail had exited into eastern Nebraska.||South central Nebraska was in the cold sector. A cold front extended from the Great Lakes southwest across Kansas. High pressure was over the Northern Plains, while a low pressure center was making its way along the front over Kansas. In the upper-levels, a broad trough extended from the Northern Rockies into the Northern Plains. A fairly potent shortwave trough was embedded within this cyclonic flow, just west of the region, and extended from Montana into Wyoming. These storms formed in an area of mid-level warm air advection around 700 mb and frontogenesis between 850 mb and 700 mb. Meanwhile, under the frontal surface, cold air advection was occurring with surface temperatures in the 50s. MUCAPE was between 500 and 1000 J/kg, and deep layer shear was around 40 knots.

Several storms produced prolific amounts of hail on this Wednesday. Thunderstorm activity was continuous, in some shape or form, from late morning all the way through midnight. The hailers began early, as a short line segment of individual storms developed over Custer and Dawson counties. The southernmost storms in this line produced some one inch hail over northeast Dawson and northwest Buffalo counties just prior to noon. Those storm gradually dissipated during the early afternoon hours, but other very isolated storms did form behind the dissipating storms through mid-afternoon. By 5 p.m. CST, three discrete storms were in progress over parts of Valley, Sherman, and Buffalo counties. They had intensified, become supercells, and were severe. These storms propagated southeastward over the following couple hours, affecting parts of Howard, Hall, and Hamilton counties as well. Each of these storms produced swaths of hail up to the size of golf balls. Some storms produced so much hail that the ground appeared white. A mesonet site in Farwell, in western Howard County, measured winds of 59 MPH as well. Another line of multicell storms formed from northwest to southeast behind these storms. The strongest storm within this line developed over Howard County around 9 p.m. CST. It tracked southeast from near St. Paul, across Aurora, and all the way down to Hebron, exiting Thayer County shortly after midnight. This storm produced one inch hail in several locations along its path. Cell training resulted in a swath of two to three inches of rain across Valley, Howard, Merrick, northern Hamilton, and York counties. The highest total was 2.90 in Scotia, in Greeley County. One of the isolated storms that developed in the middle of the afternoon was a slow-moving supercell (right-mover) that propagated into Furnas County. As this storm headed southeast, another small storm briefly developed and moved into the northwest corner of the county before dissipating. Both of these storms affected the Cambridge area between 3 p.m. and 4:45 p.m. CST, dropping two to three inches of rain. This resulted in a flash flood, with several inches of water flowing across Highway 6.||Given the sustained and persistent thunderstorm activity, the lack of forcing was very surprising. A modified continental air mass was in place. Weak high pressure was over the region to begin the day. As the high departed, a surface trough migrated into Nebraska, ahead of a strong cold front that was dropping through the Dakotas. In the upper-levels, a broad trough was over the central United States. The band of Westerlies was north of Nebraska, associated with the approaching front. There was a weak shortwave trough approaching by early evening, but it was still upstream over Montana and Wyoming. During peak heating, surface temperatures were in the 80s, with dewpoints in the upper 50s and lower 60s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor, but despite this MLCAPE varied between 1000 and 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 45-50 kts, with 0-3 km SRH 150-250 m2/s2. This shear was more than sufficient for the observed mixture of multicell and supercell convective modes.

By 5 p.m. CST, three discrete storms were in progress over parts of Valley, Sherman, and Buffalo counties. They had intensified, become supercells, and were severe. These storms propagated southeastward over the following couple hours, affecting parts of Howard, Hall, and Hamilton counties as well. Each of these storms produced swaths of hail up to the size of golf balls. Some storms produced so much hail that the ground appeared white. A mesonet site in Farwell, in western Howard County, measured winds of 59 MPH as well. Another line of multicell storms formed from northwest to southeast behind these storms. The strongest storm within this line developed over Howard County around 9 p.m. CST. It tracked southeast from near St. Paul, across Aurora, and all the way down to Hebron, exiting Thayer County shortly after midnight. This storm produced one inch hail in several locations along its path. Cell training resulted in a swath of two to three inches of rain across Valley, Howard, Merrick, northern Hamilton, and York counties. The highest total was 2.90 in Scotia, in Greeley County. One of the isolated storms that developed in the middle of the afternoon was a slow-moving supercell (right-mover) that propagated into Furnas County. As this storm headed southeast, another small storm briefly developed and moved into the northwest corner of the county before dissipating. Both of these storms affected the Cambridge area between 3 p.m. and 4:45 p.m. CST, dropping two to three inches of rain. This resulted in a flash flood, with several inches of water flowing across Highway 6.||Given the sustained and persistent thunderstorm activity, the lack of forcing was very surprising. A modified continental air mass was in place. Weak high pressure was over the region to begin the day. As the high departed, a surface trough migrated into Nebraska, ahead of a strong cold front that was dropping through the Dakotas. In the upper-levels, a broad trough was over the central United States. The band of Westerlies was north of Nebraska, associated with the approaching front. There was a weak shortwave trough approaching by early evening, but it was still upstream over Montana and Wyoming. During peak heating, surface temperatures were in the 80s, with dewpoints in the upper 50s and lower 60s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor, but despite this MLCAPE varied between 1000 and 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 45-50 kts, with 0-3 km SRH 150-250 m2/s2. This shear was more than sufficient for the observed mixture of multicell and supercell convective modes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the last of five significant severe hail events this month, in terms of size and the number of reports. Each of these events contained at least one report of 2 inch hail or greater. The uniqueness of this event was that it occurred during the morning hours on this Friday. Around 5 a.m. CST, scattered showers began to develop across much of south central Nebraska. Over the following couple hours, they increased in coverage and strengthened into thunderstorms. By 7:30 a.m. CST, several strong to severe storms were along and north of Interstate 80 from Lexington to Grand Island to Osceola. A couple of them became supercells. One supercell in particular produced most of the hail as it tracked from northern Adams County into southern Hamilton and York counties. Tennis ball to baseball size hail occurred near from near Hansen to Giltner to Trumbull. By 10 a.m. CST, it was all over. The storms hail had exited into eastern Nebraska.||South central Nebraska was in the cold sector. A cold front extended from the Great Lakes southwest across Kansas. High pressure was over the Northern Plains, while a low pressure center was making its way along the front over Kansas. In the upper-levels, a broad trough extended from the Northern Rockies into the Northern Plains. A fairly potent shortwave trough was embedded within this cyclonic flow, just west of the region, and extended from Montana into Wyoming. These storms formed in an area of mid-level warm air advection around 700 mb and frontogenesis between 850 mb and 700 mb. Meanwhile, under the frontal surface, cold air advection was occurring with surface temperatures in the 50s. MUCAPE was between 500 and 1000 J/kg, and deep layer shear was around 40 knots.

A mesonet site in Farwell, in western Howard County, measured winds of 59 MPH as well. Another line of multicell storms formed from northwest to southeast behind these storms. The strongest storm within this line developed over Howard County around 9 p.m. CST. It tracked southeast from near St. Paul, across Aurora, and all the way down to Hebron, exiting Thayer County shortly after midnight. This storm produced one inch hail in several locations along its path. Cell training resulted in a swath of two to three inches of rain across Valley, Howard, Merrick, northern Hamilton, and York counties. The highest total was 2.90 in Scotia, in Greeley County. One of the isolated storms that developed in the middle of the afternoon was a slow-moving supercell (right-mover) that propagated into Furnas County. As this storm headed southeast, another small storm briefly developed and moved into the northwest corner of the county before dissipating. Both of these storms affected the Cambridge area between 3 p.m. and 4:45 p.m. CST, dropping two to three inches of rain. This resulted in a flash flood, with several inches of water flowing across Highway 6.||Given the sustained and persistent thunderstorm activity, the lack of forcing was very surprising. A modified continental air mass was in place. Weak high pressure was over the region to begin the day. As the high departed, a surface trough migrated into Nebraska, ahead of a strong cold front that was dropping through the Dakotas. In the upper-levels, a broad trough was over the central United States. The band of Westerlies was north of Nebraska, associated with the approaching front. There was a weak shortwave trough approaching by early evening, but it was still upstream over Montana and Wyoming. During peak heating, surface temperatures were in the 80s, with dewpoints in the upper 50s and lower 60s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor, but despite this MLCAPE varied between 1000 and 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 45-50 kts, with 0-3 km SRH 150-250 m2/s2. This shear was more than sufficient for the observed mixture of multicell and supercell convective modes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Home siding was damaged in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather was widespread across south central Nebraska on this Thursday afternoon and evening, mainly south of Highway 6. This was the second day in a row of numerous thunderstorms that produce prolific amounts of severe hail. Just as with the previous day, scattered thunderstorms developed early. Some were isolated single cells while other were organized into broken lines that still exhibited some cell individuality. By mid afternoon, one of these lines had organized from west to east across most of Dawson and Buffalo counties. This line moved southeast and expanded during the subsequent hours. By 6:30 p.m. CST, the most intense portion of the line had exited into northern Kansas. There were a couple isolated storms that formed ahead of this line. Most of the severe weather occurred south of Highway 6 with these isolated cells as well as within the line, between the times of 3 and 6 p.m. CST. Several storms produced severe hail, but the largest hail occurred with discrete supercells that produced tennis ball size hail in Furnas County and three inch hail over Thayer County. Damage reported was severe with the Thayer County storm. Vehicles were dented, windows were broken, vinyl siding was damaged, and crops were decimated. There was so much hail with some of these storms, that the ground was turned white. The storm over Webster County contained strong winds which were measured as high as 69 MPH.||These storms formed along and head of a strong Canadian cold front. In the upper-levels, a trough was over the central United States, and an embedded shortwave trough was moving into the Central Plains. In the pre-storm environment, surface temperatures had climbed into the lower 80s, with dewpoints in the upper 50s and lower 60s. Mid-level lapse rates had increased from the day before and were between 7 and 7.5 degrees C/km, resulting in MLCAPE was between 1500 and 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 40-45 knots, with 0-3 km SRH 100-250 m2/s2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some wind damage occurred in Lexington on this Tuesday afternoon. Early in the afternoon, scattered showers developed over northwest Kansas. They expanded into portions of south central Nebraska as the afternoon progressed, becoming a northeast-southwest oriented line. Some of the showers intensified into what appeared to be weak thunderstorms. The storm that produced the damage in Lexington was a small storm that formed over northern Gosper County around 3:45 p.m. CST, just ahead of the line. This storm crossed the border into Dawson County and produced the damage around 4:10 p.m. CST, not long after it formed. Some large tree limbs were snapped off, resulting in power outages. One tree was uprooted at Greenwood Cemetery.||South central Nebraska was in the warm sector of low pressure located along the Montana-North Dakota border. The circulation around this low had forced the lee-side trough eastward into central South Dakota, Nebraska, and western Kansas. These showers and storms formed along this trough. In the upper-levels, southwest flow was over the Central Plains, with a trough along the Pacific Northwest Coast and a ridge over the eastern United States. The band main of Westerlies was over the west. Winds aloft over the Central Plains were fairly weak. Just before the storm moved through, the temperature at the Lexington airport was in the middle 90s with dewpoints in the 50s. Despite steep mid-levels lapse rates, the deeply mixed boundary layer limited MLCAPE to just under 1000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was marginal, approaching 30 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be near 60 MPH resulted in tree damage in the area, with some limbs upwards of 6 to 8 inches in diameter. Power outages were reported in Lexington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced an isolated, solitary severe wind gust on this Wednesday evening. A mesoscale convective vortex formed from thunderstorms over western Kansas and southwest Nebraska the prior night. This vortex headed northeast into the Nebraska Sandhills during the day. New thunderstorms developed in the sunshine in the southwest quadrant of this vortex. They expanded and marched across most of south central Nebraska, north of Interstate 80. One of these storms produced a highly localized wind gust of 67 MPH at the Central Nebraska Regional Airport in Grand Island around mid-evening. A row of plastic jersey barriers was blown over at the airport, but it was unknown if they were weighted down.||A modified continental polar air mass encompassed the United States east of the Rockies, with high pressure over eastern Canada. By late afternoon, the lee-side trough did migrate east into central Nebraska. Aloft, the Westerlies were displaced far to the north, over southern Canada. A weak trough was over Wyoming and Colorado. Temperatures were in the lower 70s and dewpoints were in the lower 60s as the storm approached Grand Island; temperatures remained cool all day under extensive cloud cover with scattered showers. MUCAPE was just under 500 J/kg and deep layer shear was around 25 knots. So, conditions were not very favorable for severe thunderstorms, but this storm still produced a severe wind gust.

This was the last of five significant severe hail events this month, in terms of size and the number of reports. Each of these events contained at least one report of 2 inch hail or greater. The uniqueness of this event was that it occurred during the morning hours on this Friday. Around 5 a.m. CST, scattered showers began to develop across much of south central Nebraska. Over the following couple hours, they increased in coverage and strengthened into thunderstorms. By 7:30 a.m. CST, several strong to severe storms were along and north of Interstate 80 from Lexington to Grand Island to Osceola. A couple of them became supercells. One supercell in particular produced most of the hail as it tracked from northern Adams County into southern Hamilton and York counties. Tennis ball to baseball size hail occurred near from near Hansen to Giltner to Trumbull. By 10 a.m. CST, it was all over. The storms hail had exited into eastern Nebraska.||South central Nebraska was in the cold sector. A cold front extended from the Great Lakes southwest across Kansas. High pressure was over the Northern Plains, while a low pressure center was making its way along the front over Kansas. In the upper-levels, a broad trough extended from the Northern Rockies into the Northern Plains. A fairly potent shortwave trough was embedded within this cyclonic flow, just west of the region, and extended from Montana into Wyoming. These storms formed in an area of mid-level warm air advection around 700 mb and frontogenesis between 850 mb and 700 mb. Meanwhile, under the frontal surface, cold air advection was occurring with surface temperatures in the 50s. MUCAPE was between 500 and 1000 J/kg, and deep layer shear was around 40 knots.

Moisture pooled along a stalled frontal boundary near the Kansas and Nebraska border. The moisture combined with steep lapse rates to create moderate instability along this boundary during the afternoon and evening hours. Scattered thunderstorms developed along this boundary by mid afternoon and continued into the evening hours as an upper level disturbance moved into the central Plains. One storm in particular moved along the Kansas and Nebraska border and produced sporadic significant severe in the form of very large hail and damaging winds.

Scattered thunderstorms developed along this boundary by mid afternoon and continued into the evening hours as an upper level disturbance moved into the central Plains. One storm in particular moved along the Kansas and Nebraska border and produced sporadic significant severe in the form of very large hail and damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures started the month of September across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. As the upper level ridge of high pressure started to break down as a storm system moved across the northern Plains, a cold front moved into the area. With the hot and humid conditions ahead of the front, strong instability developed across the area. Thunderstorms developed along the front during the late afternoon and continued into the overnight hours. Some of these storms were severe producing large hail and damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture returned to eastern Nebraska ahead of a slow moving cold front. The increasing low-level moisture combined with cool temperatures aloft to create moderate instability across the area during the afternoon and into the overnight hours. Seasonably strong shear combined with the developing instability to create an atmosphere favorable for isolated severe weather. Thunderstorms developed over northeast Nebraska by mid afternoon and slowly sank southward through the evening and overnight hours, sporadically producing severe weather.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of upper level low pressure slowly moved across the central Plains during the September 22nd through the 23rd time frame. Significant moisture was in place across the mid Missouri River Valley ahead of this system. This combination led to scattered to numerous thunderstorms over eastern Nebraska and western Iowa starting on the 22nd and continuing into the day on the 23rd. The heavy persistent thunderstorms, especially late in the night and into the early morning hours on the 23rd led to significant flash flooding across parts of western Iowa, specifically in Council Bluffs.

Hail at least the size of tennis balls fell over Thayer County on this Labor Day Monday. Scattered elevated showers, and a few embedded, weak thunderstorms, progressed through south central Nebraska during the morning and afternoon hours. Much of the area was cloudy through early afternoon, as low stratus lingered from the nighttime hours. Between noon and 1 p.m. CST, the southernmost thunderstorm emerged into Webster County. This storm rapidly intensified and became an isolated, classic supercell as it emerged from an area of cloudiness into sunshine and moved crossed Nuckolls and Thayer Counties. This area was the only portion of south central Nebraska that had seen prolonged sunshine. This storm produced very large hail over Thayer County, and those reports were on the fringe of the most intense core. Given the impressive radar signatures, hail larger than tennis balls may have fallen from west-to-east across central Thayer County. However, the core tracked just north of Highway 136, where population is extremely low. As the storm skirted to the north of Hebron, some wind damage occurred associated with the rear flank downdraft. This storm occurred early in the afternoon and exited Thayer county by 230 p.m. CST. Between 4 and 9 p.m. CST, other storms developed over south central Nebraska. While most were not severe, one storm that developed over Adams County produced hail up to the size of golf balls in Hastings.||South central Nebraska was behind a weak cold front that had moved through Sunday. As the day progressed, this front appeared to lift north back into Nebraska, as differential heating occurred between the cloudy and sunny areas. The equipment at the Hebron airport measured temperatures in the upper 80s and dewpoints in the lower 70s within the storm inflow. Combined with mid-level lapse rates between 7 and 7.5 deg C/km, the resulting MLCAPE was around 2000 J/kg. 0-3 km SRH was between 200 and 300 m2/s2, with deep layer shear 45 to 50 knots. Shear and instability were similar into the evening hours, even though temperatures had cooled into the middle 70s. In the upper-levels, a low was over the Canadian Prairies with a broad trough underneath over the Northern Rockies and Northern Plains. Nebraska was on the southern fringe of the Westerlies.

Several storms produced prolific amounts of hail on this Wednesday. Thunderstorm activity was continuous, in some shape or form, from late morning all the way through midnight. The hailers began early, as a short line segment of individual storms developed over Custer and Dawson counties. The southernmost storms in this line produced some one inch hail over northeast Dawson and northwest Buffalo counties just prior to noon. Those storm gradually dissipated during the early afternoon hours, but other very isolated storms did form behind the dissipating storms through mid-afternoon. By 5 p.m. CST, three discrete storms were in progress over parts of Valley, Sherman, and Buffalo counties. They had intensified, become supercells, and were severe. These storms propagated southeastward over the following couple hours, affecting parts of Howard, Hall, and Hamilton counties as well. Each of these storms produced swaths of hail up to the size of golf balls. Some storms produced so much hail that the ground appeared white. A mesonet site in Farwell, in western Howard County, measured winds of 59 MPH as well. Another line of multicell storms formed from northwest to southeast behind these storms. The strongest storm within this line developed over Howard County around 9 p.m. CST. It tracked southeast from near St. Paul, across Aurora, and all the way down to Hebron, exiting Thayer County shortly after midnight. This storm produced one inch hail in several locations along its path. Cell training resulted in a swath of two to three inches of rain across Valley, Howard, Merrick, northern Hamilton, and York counties. The highest total was 2.90 in Scotia, in Greeley County. One of the isolated storms that developed in the middle of the afternoon was a slow-moving supercell (right-mover) that propagated into Furnas County. As this storm headed southeast, another small storm briefly developed and moved into the northwest corner of the county before dissipating. Both of these storms affected the Cambridge area between 3 p.m. and 4:45 p.m. CST, dropping two to three inches of rain. This resulted in a flash flood, with several inches of water flowing across Highway 6.||Given the sustained and persistent thunderstorm activity, the lack of forcing was very surprising. A modified continental air mass was in place. Weak high pressure was over the region to begin the day. As the high departed, a surface trough migrated into Nebraska, ahead of a strong cold front that was dropping through the Dakotas. In the upper-levels, a broad trough was over the central United States. The band of Westerlies was north of Nebraska, associated with the approaching front. There was a weak shortwave trough approaching by early evening, but it was still upstream over Montana and Wyoming. During peak heating, surface temperatures were in the 80s, with dewpoints in the upper 50s and lower 60s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor, but despite this MLCAPE varied between 1000 and 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 45-50 kts, with 0-3 km SRH 150-250 m2/s2. This shear was more than sufficient for the observed mixture of multicell and supercell convective modes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms develop during the early morning hours of September 14th associated with a nocturnal low-level jet and moisture return to the central and northern Plains. Although the far majority of these remained below severe limits, one thunderstorm briefing became severe during the morning before all the thunderstorms dissipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The report indicated that the hail lasted for 10 min in duration.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent short-wave trough moving across the northern Plains allowed for a cold front to sweep through eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the morning on September 18th. Ahead of the cold front warm and moist was pulled northward on a strong low level jet. Scattered showers and thunderstorms occurred overnight, but as the system approached during the morning, these thunderstorms increased in coverage and intensity as they moved east producing mainly large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storms produced prolific amounts of hail on this Wednesday. Thunderstorm activity was continuous, in some shape or form, from late morning all the way through midnight. The hailers began early, as a short line segment of individual storms developed over Custer and Dawson counties. The southernmost storms in this line produced some one inch hail over northeast Dawson and northwest Buffalo counties just prior to noon. Those storm gradually dissipated during the early afternoon hours, but other very isolated storms did form behind the dissipating storms through mid-afternoon. By 5 p.m. CST, three discrete storms were in progress over parts of Valley, Sherman, and Buffalo counties. They had intensified, become supercells, and were severe. These storms propagated southeastward over the following couple hours, affecting parts of Howard, Hall, and Hamilton counties as well. Each of these storms produced swaths of hail up to the size of golf balls. Some storms produced so much hail that the ground appeared white. A mesonet site in Farwell, in western Howard County, measured winds of 59 MPH as well. Another line of multicell storms formed from northwest to southeast behind these storms. The strongest storm within this line developed over Howard County around 9 p.m. CST. It tracked southeast from near St. Paul, across Aurora, and all the way down to Hebron, exiting Thayer County shortly after midnight. This storm produced one inch hail in several locations along its path. Cell training resulted in a swath of two to three inches of rain across Valley, Howard, Merrick, northern Hamilton, and York counties. The highest total was 2.90 in Scotia, in Greeley County. One of the isolated storms that developed in the middle of the afternoon was a slow-moving supercell (right-mover) that propagated into Furnas County. As this storm headed southeast, another small storm briefly developed and moved into the northwest corner of the county before dissipating. Both of these storms affected the Cambridge area between 3 p.m. and 4:45 p.m. CST, dropping two to three inches of rain. This resulted in a flash flood, with several inches of water flowing across Highway 6.||Given the sustained and persistent thunderstorm activity, the lack of forcing was very surprising. A modified continental air mass was in place. Weak high pressure was over the region to begin the day. As the high departed, a surface trough migrated into Nebraska, ahead of a strong cold front that was dropping through the Dakotas. In the upper-levels, a broad trough was over the central United States. The band of Westerlies was north of Nebraska, associated with the approaching front. There was a weak shortwave trough approaching by early evening, but it was still upstream over Montana and Wyoming. During peak heating, surface temperatures were in the 80s, with dewpoints in the upper 50s and lower 60s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor, but despite this MLCAPE varied between 1000 and 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 45-50 kts, with 0-3 km SRH 150-250 m2/s2. This shear was more than sufficient for the observed mixture of multicell and supercell convective modes.

In the upper-levels, a broad trough was over the central United States. The band of Westerlies was north of Nebraska, associated with the approaching front. There was a weak shortwave trough approaching by early evening, but it was still upstream over Montana and Wyoming. During peak heating, surface temperatures were in the 80s, with dewpoints in the upper 50s and lower 60s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor, but despite this MLCAPE varied between 1000 and 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 45-50 kts, with 0-3 km SRH 150-250 m2/s2. This shear was more than sufficient for the observed mixture of multicell and supercell convective modes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storms produced prolific amounts of hail on this Wednesday. Thunderstorm activity was continuous, in some shape or form, from late morning all the way through midnight. The hailers began early, as a short line segment of individual storms developed over Custer and Dawson counties. The southernmost storms in this line produced some one inch hail over northeast Dawson and northwest Buffalo counties just prior to noon. Those storm gradually dissipated during the early afternoon hours, but other very isolated storms did form behind the dissipating storms through mid-afternoon. By 5 p.m. CST, three discrete storms were in progress over parts of Valley, Sherman, and Buffalo counties. They had intensified, become supercells, and were severe. These storms propagated southeastward over the following couple hours, affecting parts of Howard, Hall, and Hamilton counties as well. Each of these storms produced swaths of hail up to the size of golf balls. Some storms produced so much hail that the ground appeared white. A mesonet site in Farwell, in western Howard County, measured winds of 59 MPH as well. Another line of multicell storms formed from northwest to southeast behind these storms. The strongest storm within this line developed over Howard County around 9 p.m. CST. It tracked southeast from near St. Paul, across Aurora, and all the way down to Hebron, exiting Thayer County shortly after midnight. This storm produced one inch hail in several locations along its path. Cell training resulted in a swath of two to three inches of rain across Valley, Howard, Merrick, northern Hamilton, and York counties. The highest total was 2.90 in Scotia, in Greeley County. One of the isolated storms that developed in the middle of the afternoon was a slow-moving supercell (right-mover) that propagated into Furnas County. As this storm headed southeast, another small storm briefly developed and moved into the northwest corner of the county before dissipating. Both of these storms affected the Cambridge area between 3 p.m. and 4:45 p.m. CST, dropping two to three inches of rain. This resulted in a flash flood, with several inches of water flowing across Highway 6.||Given the sustained and persistent thunderstorm activity, the lack of forcing was very surprising. Severe weather was widespread across south central Nebraska on this Thursday afternoon and evening, mainly south of Highway 6. This was the second day in a row of numerous thunderstorms that produce prolific amounts of severe hail. Just as with the previous day, scattered thunderstorms developed early. Some were isolated single cells while other were organized into broken lines that still exhibited some cell individuality. By mid afternoon, one of these lines had organized from west to east across most of Dawson and Buffalo counties. This line moved southeast and expanded during the subsequent hours. By 6:30 p.m. CST, the most intense portion of the line had exited into northern Kansas. There were a couple isolated storms that formed ahead of this line. Most of the severe weather occurred south of Highway 6 with these isolated cells as well as within the line, between the times of 3 and 6 p.m. CST. Several storms produced severe hail, but the largest hail occurred with discrete supercells that produced tennis ball size hail in Furnas County and three inch hail over Thayer County. Damage reported was severe with the Thayer County storm. Vehicles were dented, windows were broken, vinyl siding was damaged, and crops were decimated. There was so much hail with some of these storms, that the ground was turned white. The storm over Webster County contained strong winds which were measured as high as 69 MPH.||These storms formed along and head of a strong Canadian cold front. In the upper-levels, a trough was over the central United States, and an embedded shortwave trough was moving into the Central Plains. In the pre-storm environment, surface temperatures had climbed into the lower 80s, with dewpoints in the upper 50s and lower 60s. Mid-level lapse rates had increased from the day before and were between 7 and 7.5 degrees C/km, resulting in MLCAPE was between 1500 and 2000 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 40-45 knots, with 0-3 km SRH 100-250 m2/s2.

Severe weather was widespread across south central Nebraska on this Thursday afternoon and evening, mainly south of Highway 6. This was the second day in a row of numerous thunderstorms that produce prolific amounts of severe hail. Just as with the previous day, scattered thunderstorms developed early. Some were isolated single cells while other were organized into broken lines that still exhibited some cell individuality. By mid afternoon, one of these lines had organized from west to east across most of Dawson and Buffalo counties. This line moved southeast and expanded during the subsequent hours. By 6:30 p.m. CST, the most intense portion of the line had exited into northern Kansas. There were a couple isolated storms that formed ahead of this line. Most of the severe weather occurred south of Highway 6 with these isolated cells as well as within the line, between the times of 3 and 6 p.m. CST. Several storms produced severe hail, but the largest hail occurred with discrete supercells that produced tennis ball size hail in Furnas County and three inch hail over Thayer County. Damage reported was severe with the Thayer County storm. Vehicles were dented, windows were broken, vinyl siding was damaged, and crops were decimated. There was so much hail with some of these storms, that the ground was turned white. A potent short-wave trough moving across the northern Plains allowed for a cold front to sweep through eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the morning on September 18th. Ahead of the cold front warm and moist was pulled northward on a strong low level jet. Scattered showers and thunderstorms occurred overnight, but as the system approached during the morning, these thunderstorms increased in coverage and intensity as they moved east producing mainly large hail.

A cold front slowly moving through eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa produced a couple of isolated thunderstorms during the evening on September 17th. The thunderstorms, largely non-severe, persisted for a couple of hours during the evening before the cold front pushed into northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri ending the thunderstorm threat.

Very hot temperatures started the month of September across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. As the upper level ridge of high pressure started to break down as a storm system moved across the northern Plains, a cold front moved into the area. With the hot and humid conditions ahead of the front, strong instability developed across the area. Thunderstorms developed along the front during the late afternoon and continued into the overnight hours. Some of these storms were severe producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of large hail, some as big a tennis balls were received, mainly from social media, but also from spotters within the city of Lincoln. The largest hail fell on the southeast side of the city.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports were received from social media and the broadcast media of wind damage in the city of Lincoln. The damage was mainly to trees, but some structural damage was also reported to a manufactured home.

An area of upper level low pressure slowly moved across the central Plains during the September 22nd through the 23rd time frame. Significant moisture was in place across the mid Missouri River Valley ahead of this system. This combination led to scattered to numerous thunderstorms over eastern Nebraska and western Iowa starting on the 22nd and continuing into the day on the 23rd. The heavy persistent thunderstorms, especially late in the night and into the early morning hours on the 23rd led to significant flash flooding across parts of western Iowa, specifically in Council Bluffs.

This was the last of five significant severe hail events this month, in terms of size and the number of reports. Each of these events contained at least one report of 2 inch hail or greater. The uniqueness of this event was that it occurred during the morning hours on this Friday. Around 5 a.m. CST, scattered showers began to develop across much of south central Nebraska. Over the following couple hours, they increased in coverage and strengthened into thunderstorms. By 7:30 a.m. CST, several strong to severe storms were along and north of Interstate 80 from Lexington to Grand Island to Osceola. A couple of them became supercells. One supercell in particular produced most of the hail as it tracked from northern Adams County into southern Hamilton and York counties. Tennis ball to baseball size hail occurred near from near Hansen to Giltner to Trumbull. By 10 a.m. CST, it was all over. The storms hail had exited into eastern Nebraska.||South central Nebraska was in the cold sector. A cold front extended from the Great Lakes southwest across Kansas. High pressure was over the Northern Plains, while a low pressure center was making its way along the front over Kansas. In the upper-levels, a broad trough extended from the Northern Rockies into the Northern Plains. A fairly potent shortwave trough was embedded within this cyclonic flow, just west of the region, and extended from Montana into Wyoming. These storms formed in an area of mid-level warm air advection around 700 mb and frontogenesis between 850 mb and 700 mb. Meanwhile, under the frontal surface, cold air advection was occurring with surface temperatures in the 50s. MUCAPE was between 500 and 1000 J/kg, and deep layer shear was around 40 knots.

Isolated thunderstorms developed over the Nebraska Panhandle during the early evening hours of July 27th.  They developed in the vicinity of a surface trough of low pressure, and moved to the east northeast into the western Nebraska Sandhills and portions of the northeastern Nebraska Panhandle. Severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts of 70 MPH estimated at this location.
